79:40.arabic: وأما من خاف مقام ربه ونهى النفس عن الهوى

79:42.text: They ask you about the Hour, and when it will take place!

79:43.text: It is not you (Muhammad) who is destined to announce its time.

79:46.text: The day they see it, they will feel as if they lasted one evening or half a day.
79:46.arabic: كأنهم يوم يرونها لم يلبثوا إلا عشية أو ضحىها

question: Explain Surah 52 (Mount Sinai) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 52 (Aṭ-Ṭūr — "Mount Sinai") is forty-nine verses, Meccan. The surah opens with a six-element oath sequence anchored on the Mount of Sinai and proceeds through certainty of judgment, paradise rewards, and one of the Quran's tightest anti-Hadith verses ([52:34]).

**The six oaths (52:1–6).**

[52:1]: "Mt. Sinai." — *wa-ṭ-Ṭūr*. The mountain of Moses's audience. The Quran swears upon a *geographical sacred site*.

[52:2]: "The recorded scripture." — *wa-kitābin masṭūr*. *Masṭūr* = "lined, written in rows."

[52:3]: "Published in books." — *fī raqqin manshūr*. *Raqq* = parchment / vellum; *manshūr* = unrolled, published.

[52:4]: "The frequented Shrine." — *wa-l-bayti l-ma`mūr*. Classical exegesis: a heavenly counterpart of the Kaaba, perpetually visited by angels.

[52:5]: "The exalted ceiling." — *wa-s-saqfi l-marfū`*. The raised sky.

[52:6]: "The sea that is set aflame." — *wa-l-baḥri l-masjūr*. *Masjūr* = "kindled, boiled, set ablaze." The image — *a sea on fire* — both metaphorical (the resurrection-day sea of [81:6]) and (in some Khalifa-aligned readings) anticipatory of physical-cosmological events.

**The verdict.**

[52:7]: "Your Lord's requital is unavoidable." — *inna `adhāba rabbika la-wāqi`*. *Wāqi`* = "falling, due to occur."

[52:8]: "No force in the universe can stop it." — *mā lahū min dāfi`*.

**The cosmic dissolution.**

[52:9]–[52:10]: "The day the sky violently thunders. The mountains will be wiped out." — sky thunders, mountains move.

[52:11]: "Woe on that day to the disbelievers" — *fa-waylun yawma'idhin li-l-mukadhdhibīn*.

**Hell scene (52:13–16).**

[52:13]: "They will be herded into Gehenna, forcibly." — *yuda``ūna ilā nāri jahannama da``ā*. *Da``a* = to push, to thrust.

[52:14]: "This is the Fire in which you used to disbelieve." — addressed directly.

[52:15]–[52:16]: "Is this magic, or do you not see? Suffer the burning. Whether you are patient or impatient, it will be the same for you." — the disbelievers' patience or impatience is *equally irrelevant* in hell.

**Paradise scene (52:17–28).**

[52:17]: "The righteous have deserved gardens and bliss." — *inna l-muttaqīna fī jannātin wa-na`īm*.

[52:18]: "They enjoy what their Lord has reserved for them; their Lord has spared them the retribution of Hell."

[52:21]: "For those who believed, and their children also followed them in belief, we will have their children join them. We never fail to reward them for any work. Every person is paid for what he did." — the *familial reunion* clause: believing families are joined together. Each person is paid for *their own works* ([52:21]'s second half) — combining the dynastic-blessing and individual-responsibility motifs in one verse.

[52:23]: "They will enjoy drinks that are never polluted, and never sinful to drink." — *kā'san lā laghwun fīhā wa-lā ta'thīm*. The paradise-drinks produce no *laghw* (vain speech) and no *ithm* (sinful effect).

**The Prophet's defense (52:29–34).**

[52:29]: "You shall remind the people. With your Lord's blessing's upon you, you are neither a soothsayer, nor crazy." — *bi-ni`mati rabbika mā anta bi-kāhinin wa-lā majnūn*.

[52:30]: "They may say, 'He is a poet; let us just wait until he is dead.'" — the disbelievers' three labels (*kāhin, majnūn, shā`ir* — soothsayer, crazy, poet) are systematically refuted.

[52:33]: "Do they say, 'He made it all up?' Instead, they are simply disbelievers." — the *iftirā'* accusation refuted: their problem is disbelief, not the Quran's fabricated-ness.

[52:34]: "Let them produce a Hadith like this, if they are truthful." — *fa-l-ya'tū bi-ḥadīthin mithlihī in kānū ṣādiqīn*. In Khalifa's exegesis, this verse is paired with [69:43]–[69:47] and [77:50] as the *anti-Hadith trilogy*: the Quran challenges its opponents to produce *a single ḥadīth like this one*, and they cannot — therefore the Quran is *the only authoritative ḥadīth* (saying / report). Section header in Khalifa's translation: *"Mohammedans"*, signalling his concern about post-Quranic *ḥadīth*-based religion.

**The series of rhetorical challenges (52:35–43).**

A run of nine *am* ("or, indeed") questions: *am khuliqū min ghayri shay'in / am hum-u l-khāliqūn / am khalaqū s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍ / am `indahum khazā'inu rabbika / am hum-u l-muṣayṭirūn / am lahum sullamun yastami`ūna fīh / am lahu l-banātu wa-lakum-u l-banūn / am tas'aluhum ajran fa-hum min maghramin muthqalūn / am `indahum-u l-ghaybu fa-hum yaktubūn*. Are they created from nothing? Are they the creators? Did they create the heavens? Do they have the treasures? Are they in control? Do they have a ladder to listen up? Are daughters His and sons theirs? Are you charging them a wage? Do they have the unseen knowledge?

[52:43]: "Do they have another god beside GOD? GOD be glorified, far above having partners." — the closing rhetorical challenge: *do they have another god*?

**The closing.**

[52:48]: "You shall steadfastly persevere in carrying out your Lord's command — you are in our eyes — and glorify and praise your Lord when you get up." — *innaka bi-a`yuninā*, "you are in our eyes" — under direct watch.

[52:49]: "Also during the night glorify Him, and at dawn as the stars fade away." — the closing *temporal pattern* for the Prophet's worship: rising, night, dawn-as-stars-fade.

**Synthesis.**

The surah swears on Sinai, on the recorded scripture, on the heavenly shrine, on the raised sky, on the sea-set-aflame; declares God's requital unstoppable; describes the dual scenes of hell and paradise; refutes the *kāhin/majnūn/shā`ir/muftarī* labels for the Prophet ([52:29], [52:30], [52:33]); issues the anti-Hadith challenge ([52:34]); runs a barrage of nine rhetorical challenges on the disbelievers' position ([52:35]–[52:43]); and closes with the Prophet's worship schedule under the watching divine eyes ([52:48]–[52:49]).

52:6.text: The sea that is set aflame.

52:8.text: No force in the universe can stop it.

52:9.text: The day will come when the sky will violently thunder.

52:11.text: Woe on that day to the disbelievers -

52:17.text: The righteous have deserved gardens and bliss.

52:18.text: They enjoy what their Lord has reserved for them; their Lord has spared them the retribution of Hell.
52:18.arabic: فكهين بما ءاتىهم ربهم ووقىهم ربهم عذاب الجحيم

52:21.text: For those who believed, and their children also followed them in belief, we will have their children join them. We never fail to reward them for any work. Every person is paid for what he did.
52:21.arabic: والذين ءامنوا واتبعتهم ذريتهم بإيمن ألحقنا بهم ذريتهم وما ألتنهم من عملهم من شيء كل امري بما كسب رهين

52:23.text: They will enjoy drinks that are never polluted, and never sinful to drink.
52:23.arabic: يتنزعون فيها كأسا لا لغو فيها ولا تأثيم

52:29.text: You shall remind the people. With your Lord's blessing's upon you, you are neither a soothsayer, nor crazy.
52:29.arabic: فذكر فما أنت بنعمت ربك بكاهن ولا مجنون

52:30.text: They may say, "He is a poet; let us just wait until he is dead."
52:30.arabic: أم يقولون شاعر نتربص به ريب المنون

52:33.text: Do they say, "He made it all up?" Instead, they are simply disbelievers. "Mohammedans"
52:33.arabic: أم يقولون تقوله بل لا يؤمنون

52:34.text: Let them produce a Hadith like this, if they are truthful.
52:34.arabic: فليأتوا بحديث مثله إن كانوا صدقين

52:38.text: Do they climb a ladder that enables them to listen? Let their listeners show their proof.
52:38.arabic: أم لهم سلم يستمعون فيه فليأت مستمعهم بسلطن مبين

52:43.text: Do they have another god beside GOD? GOD be glorified, far above having partners.
52:43.arabic: أم لهم إله غير الله سبحن الله عما يشركون

52:48.text: You shall steadfastly persevere in carrying out your Lord's command - you are in our eyes - and glorify and praise your Lord when you get up.
52:48.arabic: واصبر لحكم ربك فإنك بأعيننا وسبح بحمد ربك حين تقوم

52:49.text: Also during the night glorify Him, and at dawn as the stars fade away.

question: What is the central message of Surah 32 (Prostration)?
answer:
Surah 32 (As-Sajdah — "Prostration") is thirty verses, Meccan. The surah is named after [32:15], which is a *sajdah* (prostration) verse — its recitation triggers a bodily prostration. The surah's central message is: *the Quran is from God, creation is purposive, the human is structurally a worshiper, and the small retribution of this world is meant to recall us to the great one before it arrives*.

**The opening — Alif Lam Mim and the scripture's authority.**

[32:1]: "A. L. M." — *Alif Lām Mīm*. One of the muqaṭṭa`āt; Khalifa traces it as part of the Code-19 framework's *initials* set.

[32:2]: "The book is, without a doubt, a revelation from the Lord of the universe." — *tanzīlu l-kitābi lā rayba fīhi min rabbi l-`ālamīn*.

[32:3]: "They said, 'He fabricated it.' Indeed, this is the truth from your Lord, to warn people who never received a warner before you, that they may be guided." — refutes the fabrication charge; identifies the Arab audience as one *never previously warned*.

**The cosmological argument.**

[32:4]: "GOD is the One who created the heavens and the earth, and everything between them in six days, then assumed all authority. You have none beside Him as Lord, nor do you have an intercessor. Would you not take heed?" — the *six days* and *no intercessor* clauses are both load-bearing.

[32:5]: "All matters are controlled by Him from the heaven to the earth. To Him, the day is equivalent to one thousand of your years." — *yawmun kāna miqdāruhū alfa sanatin mimmā ta`uddūn*. A *cosmological time-scale* — used in Khalifa's framework to soften any literalist insistence on the six "days" being twenty-four-hour periods.

**The creation of the human.**

[32:7]–[32:9]: "He is the One who perfected everything He created, and started the creation of the human from clay. Then He continued his reproduction through a certain lowly liquid. He shaped him and blew into him from His spirit. And He gave you the hearing, the eyesight, and the brains; rarely are you thankful." — three stages: (a) first human from *clay*, (b) descendants from *a despicable fluid*, (c) shaping + *spirit-breath*. The three senses given are *hearing, sight, brains* — the Quran's standard cognitive triad.

**The death scene.**

[32:11]: "Say, 'You will be put to death by the angel in whose charge you are placed, then to your Lord you will be returned.'" — *malak al-mawt*, "the angel of death" — assigned to each soul.

**The judgment dialogue.**

[32:12]: "If only you could see the guilty when they bow down their heads before their Lord: 'Our Lord, now we have seen and we have heard. Send us back and we will be righteous. Now we have attained certainty.'" — *the disbelievers' post-mortem confession*. They ask to be returned; the next verse refuses.

[32:13]: "Had we willed, we could have given every soul its guidance, but it is already predetermined that I will fill Hell with jinns and humans, all together." — one of the Quran's most theologically dense verses: *guidance could have been universal*, but the *cosmic pre-decision* allows the divide.

**The *sajdah* verse — the surah's anchor.**

[32:15]: "The only people who truly believe in our revelations are those who fall prostrate upon hearing them. They glorify and praise their Lord, without any arrogance." — *innamā yu'minu bi-āyātinā lladhīna idhā dhukkirū bihā kharrū sujjadan*. The *behavioral signature* of belief: prostration on hearing the revelation. This verse is one of the canonical *sajdah* verses — recitation triggers a bodily prostration.

**The believer's life.**

[32:16]: "Their sides readily forsake their beds, in order to worship their Lord, out of reverence and hope, and from our provisions to them, they give." — *tatajāfā junūbuhum `ani l-maḍāji`*. The believer's *side leaves the bed* — a metonym for *night-prayer (tahajjud)*. The pair: reverence + hope (*khawfan wa-ṭama`an*); and *they give from our provisions*.

[32:17]: "You have no idea how much joy and happiness are waiting for you as a reward for your (righteous) works." — *fa-lā ta`lamu nafsun mā ukhfiya lahum min qurrati a`yun*. *Qurratu a`yun* = "coolness of eyes" — Arabic idiom for *deep joy / satisfaction*.

**The not-equal claim.**

[32:18]: "Is one who is a believer the same as one who is wicked? They are not equal." — *afa-man kāna mu'minan ka-man kāna fāsiqan, lā yastawūn*. The categorical inequality of moral types.

**The pedagogical small retribution.**

[32:21]: "We let them taste the smaller retribution (of this world), before they incur the greater retribution (of the Hereafter), that they may (take a hint and) reform." — *al-`adhāb al-adnā* (the smaller, this-worldly suffering) is *designed to recall to reform* before *al-`adhāb al-akbar* (the greater, Hereafter) is incurred.

**The Mosaic continuity.**

[32:23]–[32:24]: "We have given Moses the scripture — do not harbor any doubt about meeting Him — and we made it a guide for the Children of Israel. We appointed from among them imams who guided in accordance with our commandments, because they steadfastly persevered and attained certainty about our revelations." — the *imamate* of the Children of Israel was granted *because they steadfastly persevered* and *attained certainty*. Imamate is *earned by perseverance and certainty*, not inherited.

**Central message.**

The Quran is from God ([32:2]); creation is purposive and the human is given hearing, sight, brains, and divine spirit-breath ([32:7]–[32:9]); the *behavioral signature* of belief is *prostration on hearing the revelation* ([32:15]) and *night-prayer + charity from God's provision* ([32:16]); the small retribution of this world is *meant to recall us to reform before the great one* ([32:21]); and imamate is granted to those who *persevere and attain certainty* ([32:24]).

32:2.text: The book is, without a doubt, a revelation from the Lord of the universe.
32:2.arabic: تنزيل الكتب لا ريب فيه من رب العلمين

32:4.text: GOD is the One who created the heavens and the earth, and everything between them in six days, then assumed all authority. You have none beside Him as Lord, nor do you have an intercessor. Would you not take heed?
32:4.arabic: الله الذي خلق السموت والأرض وما بينهما في ستة أيام ثم استوى على العرش ما لكم من دونه من ولي ولا شفيع أفلا تتذكرون

32:5.text: All matters are controlled by Him from the heaven to the earth. To Him, the day is equivalent to one thousand of your years.
32:5.arabic: يدبر الأمر من السماء إلى الأرض ثم يعرج إليه في يوم كان مقداره ألف سنة مما تعدون

32:7.text: He is the One who perfected everything He created, and started the creation of the human from clay.
32:7.arabic: الذي أحسن كل شيء خلقه وبدأ خلق الإنسن من طين

32:8.text: Then He continued his reproduction through a certain lowly liquid.
32:8.arabic: ثم جعل نسله من سللة من ماء مهين

32:9.text: He shaped him and blew into him from His spirit. And He gave you the hearing, the eyesight, and the brains; rarely are you thankful.
32:9.arabic: ثم سوىه ونفخ فيه من روحه وجعل لكم السمع والأبصر والأفـدة قليلا ما تشكرون

32:12.text: If only you could see the guilty when they bow down their heads before their Lord: "Our Lord, now we have seen and we have heard. Send us back and we will be righteous. Now we have attained certainty."
32:12.arabic: ولو ترى إذ المجرمون ناكسوا رءوسهم عند ربهم ربنا أبصرنا وسمعنا فارجعنا نعمل صلحا إنا موقنون

32:13.text: Had we willed, we could have given every soul its guidance, but it is already predetermined that I will fill Hell with jinns and humans, all together.
32:13.arabic: ولو شئنا لأتينا كل نفس هدىها ولكن حق القول مني لأملأن جهنم من الجنة والناس أجمعين

32:15.text: The only people who truly believe in our revelations are those who fall prostrate upon hearing them. They glorify and praise their Lord, without any arrogance.
32:15.arabic: إنما يؤمن بـايتنا الذين إذا ذكروا بها خروا سجدا وسبحوا بحمد ربهم وهم لا يستكبرون۩

32:16.text: Their sides readily forsake their beds, in order to worship their Lord, out of reverence and hope, and from our provisions to them, they give. Heaven: Indescribably Beautiful
32:16.arabic: تتجافى جنوبهم عن المضاجع يدعون ربهم خوفا وطمعا ومما رزقنهم ينفقون

32:17.text: You have no idea how much joy and happiness are waiting for you as a reward for your (righteous) works.
32:17.arabic: فلا تعلم نفس ما أخفي لهم من قرة أعين جزاء بما كانوا يعملون

32:18.text: Is one who is a believer the same as one who is wicked? They are not equal.
32:18.arabic: أفمن كان مؤمنا كمن كان فاسقا لا يستون

32:21.text: We let them taste the smaller retribution (of this world), before they incur the greater retribution (of the Hereafter), that they may (take a hint and) reform.
32:21.arabic: ولنذيقنهم من العذاب الأدنى دون العذاب الأكبر لعلهم يرجعون

32:23.text: We have given Moses the scripture - do not harbor any doubt about meeting Him - and we made it a guide for the Children of Israel.
32:23.arabic: ولقد ءاتينا موسى الكتب فلا تكن في مرية من لقائه وجعلنه هدى لبني إسرـءيل

32:24.text: We appointed from among them imams who guided in accordance with our commandments, because they steadfastly persevered and attained certainty about our revelations.
32:24.arabic: وجعلنا منهم أئمة يهدون بأمرنا لما صبروا وكانوا بـايتنا يوقنون

question: Explain Surah 89 (Dawn) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 89 (Al-Fajr — "Dawn") is thirty verses, Meccan. The surah opens with a five-element oath sequence, surveys three destroyed ancient peoples, exposes the human's habit of mis-interpreting prosperity and poverty as divine verdicts, and closes with one of the Quran's most quoted death-bed invitations to the contented soul.

**The five oaths (89:1–5).**

[89:1]: "By the dawn." — *wa-l-fajr*.

[89:2]: "And the ten nights." — *wa-layālin `ashr*. Classical exegesis identifies the *ten nights* with the first ten of Dhū al-Ḥijjah (the pilgrimage period) or the last ten of Ramadan; Khalifa preserves the indeterminate reading.

[89:3]: "By the even and the odd." — *wa-sh-shaf`i wa-l-watr*. *Shaf`* = even / pair; *watr* = odd / single. A numerical-oath that becomes central in Khalifa's Code-19 analyses.

[89:4]: "By the night as it passes." — *wa-l-layli idhā yasr*.

[89:5]: "A profound oath, for one who possesses intelligence." — *hal fī dhālika qasamun li-dhī ḥijr*. *Ḥijr* = "intellect, restraint" — *for those who possess discernment*.

**The three ancient peoples — `Aad, Thamoud, Pharaoh.**

[89:6]–[89:8]: "Have you noted what your Lord did to `Aad? `Iram of the pillars. There was nothing like it anywhere." — *Iram dhāti l-`imād, allatī lam yukhlaq mithluhā fī l-bilād*. *Iram of the pillars* — the lost-city reference unique to this surah.

[89:9]: "Also Thamoud, who carved the rocks in their valley." — *wa-thamūda lladhīna jābū ṣ-ṣakhra bi-l-wād*. The Nabataean / Mada'in Saleh imagery.

[89:10]: "And Pharaoh who possessed great armies." — *wa-fir`awna dhī l-awtād*. *Awtād* = "pegs / stakes" — usually rendered as *stakes of his throne*, signifying *firm power*.

[89:11]–[89:12]: "They all transgressed in the land. They spread evil throughout."

[89:13]: "Consequently, your Lord poured upon them a whipping retribution." — *fa-ṣabba `alayhim rabbuka sawṭa `adhāb*. *Sawṭ* = whip — the retribution is a *whipping*.

[89:14]: "Your Lord is ever watchful." — *inna rabbaka la-bi-l-mirṣād*. *Mirṣād* — "observation post" — the divine watch.

**The human's misreading of providence (89:15–20).**

[89:15]: "When the human being is tested by his Lord, through blessings and joy, he says, 'My Lord is generous towards me.'" — *fa-yaqūlu rabbī akraman*. When blessed, *I am God's favorite*.

[89:16]: "But if He tests him through reduction in provisions, he says, 'My Lord is humiliating me!'" — *fa-yaqūlu rabbī ahānan*. When deprived, *I am being humiliated*.

The Quran's *correction* of both misreadings begins in the next verse: God's tests are not direct providence-as-verdict. Wealth and poverty are *both* tests. The next verses identify the *real* moral diagnostic — not the soul's economic state, but how the soul *behaves* toward those in need:

[89:17]: "Wrong! It is you who brought it on yourselves by not regarding the orphan." — *kallā bal lā tukrimūna l-yatīm*. The *first indictment*: not honoring the orphan.

[89:18]: "And not advocating charity for the poor." — *wa-lā taḥāḍḍūna `alā ṭa`āmi l-miskīn*. The *second*: not advocating feeding of the destitute.

[89:19]: "And consuming the inheritance of helpless orphans." — *wa-ta'kulūna t-turātha aklan lammā*. Consuming inheritances *voraciously*.

[89:20]: "And loving the money too much." — *wa-tuḥibbūna l-māla ḥubban jammā*. Excessive love of wealth.

**The Day's arrival (89:21–24).**

[89:21]: "Indeed, when the earth is crushed, utterly crushed." — *kallā idhā dukkati l-arḍu dakkan dakkā*. The Day's seismic verb is *dakka* — doubled for emphasis.

[89:22]: "And your Lord comes, together with the angels in row after row." — *wa-jā'a rabbuka wa-l-malaku ṣaffan ṣaffā*.

[89:23]: "On that day, Gehenna will be brought forth." — *wa-jī'a yawma'idhin bi-jahannam*. *On that day, the human will remember — but how can the remembrance benefit him then?* ([89:23] continued).

**The contented-soul invitation (89:27–30).**

[89:27]: "As for you, O content soul." — *yā-ayyatuhā n-nafsu l-muṭma'innah*. *An-nafs al-muṭma'innah* — "the soul at peace, content" — the most-quoted phrase of the surah.

[89:28]: "Return to your Lord, pleased and pleasing." — *irji`ī ilā rabbiki rāḍiyatan marḍiyyah*. *Pleased and pleasing* — a perfect-mutual-pleasure formula.

[89:29]: "Welcome into My servants." — *fa-dkhulī fī `ibādī*. "Enter among My servants."

[89:30]: "Welcome into My Paradise." — *wa-dkhulī jannatī*. "Enter My garden."

**Synthesis.**

The oaths of dawn, ten nights, even and odd, passing night ([89:1]–[89:5]) load the conclusion: *intelligent people read this seriously*. Three destroyed nations — `Aad of the pillars, Thamoud of the carved rocks, Pharaoh of the pegs — are listed as examples ([89:6]–[89:14]). The human misreads wealth as favor and poverty as humiliation ([89:15]–[89:16]) — but the *real* diagnostic is treatment of the orphan, advocacy for the poor, fair handling of inheritance, and proportionate love of money ([89:17]–[89:20]). On the Day, the earth is crushed and Gehenna brought forth ([89:21]–[89:23]) — and the surah closes with the *content soul* invited home to its Lord ([89:27]–[89:30]).

89:3.text: By the even and the odd.

89:4.text: By the night as it passes.

89:5.text: A profound oath, for one who possesses intelligence.
89:5.arabic: هل في ذلك قسم لذي حجر

89:6.text: Have you noted what your Lord did to `Aad?
89:6.arabic: ألم تر كيف فعل ربك بعاد

89:7.text: Erum; the town with tall buildings.

89:8.text: There was nothing like it anywhere.
89:8.arabic: التي لم يخلق مثلها في البلد

89:9.text: Also Thamoud, who carved the rocks in their valley.

89:11.text: They all transgressed in the land.

89:13.text: Consequently, your Lord poured upon them a whipping retribution.

89:15.text: When the human being is tested by his Lord, through blessings and joy, he says, "My Lord is generous towards me."
89:15.arabic: فأما الإنسن إذا ما ابتلىه ربه فأكرمه ونعمه فيقول ربي أكرمن

89:16.text: But if He tests him through reduction in provisions, he says, "My Lord is humiliating me!"
89:16.arabic: وأما إذا ما ابتلىه فقدر عليه رزقه فيقول ربي أهنن

89:17.text: Wrong! It is you who brought it on yourselves by not regarding the orphan.

89:18.text: And not advocating charity towards the poor.

89:19.text: And consuming the inheritance of helpless orphans.

89:20.text: And loving the money too much.

89:21.text: Indeed, when the earth is crushed, utterly crushed.
89:21.arabic: كلا إذا دكت الأرض دكا دكا

89:22.text: And your Lord comes, together with the angels in row after row.

89:23.text: On that day, Gehenna will be brought forth. On that day, the human being will remember - but what a remembrance - it will be too late.
89:23.arabic: وجايء يومئذ بجهنم يومئذ يتذكر الإنسن وأنى له الذكرى

89:25.text: On that day, no retribution could be worse than His retribution.

89:27.text: As for you, O content soul.

89:28.text: Return to your Lord, pleased and pleasing.

question: Explain Surah 67 (Kingship) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 67 (Al-Mulk — "Kingship") is thirty verses, Meccan. Khalifa's translation titles it "Kingship" because verse 1 names God as the One *in whose hand* is *al-mulk* — all sovereignty. The surah is one of the most-recited of the short night-prayer surahs in Sunni tradition; its content is a tight chain of *look-and-test* invitations addressed to the audience's eyes, ears, and bird-instinct.

**The opening claim — sovereignty.**

[67:1]: "Most exalted is the One in whose hands is all kingship, and He is Omnipotent." — *tabāraka lladhī bi-yadihi l-mulk*. *Tabāraka* = "exalted, blessed, abundant in goodness." *Bi-yadihi l-mulk* — "in His hand is the kingship/sovereignty."

[67:2]: "The One who created death and life for the purpose of distinguishing those among you who would do better. He is the Almighty, the Forgiving." — *liyabluwakum ayyukum aḥsanu `amalā*. The purpose-clause: *death and life are created to test which of you is better in deed*. The pair *death and life* is theologically dense: *al-mawt* is mentioned *first* — death is, in the Quranic frame, prior to life as part of the creation chain.

**The look-test architecture — three rounds.**

[67:3]: "He created seven universes in layers. You do not see any imperfection in the creation by the Most Gracious. Keep looking; do you see any flaw?" — *fa-rji`i l-baṣar, hal tarā min fuṭūr*. *Fuṭūr* = "cracks, splits, flaws." The first imperative *return your gaze* (*irji`i l-baṣar*) — *do you see any crack*?

[67:4]: "Look again and again; your eyes will come back stumped and conquered." — *thumma rji`i l-baṣara karratayn yanqalib ilayka l-baṣaru khāsi'an wa-huwa ḥasīr*. *Khāsi'* = stumped, defeated; *ḥasīr* = exhausted. The eye's *defeat* is the proof of *the universe's seamlessness*.

[67:5]: "We adorned the lowest universe with lamps, and guarded its borders with projectiles against the devils; we prepared for them a retribution in Hell." — the lower heaven's *lamps* are the stars; *projectiles* are the meteors (cross-reference [72:8]–[72:9]).

**The hell-dialogue (67:7–11).**

[67:7]: "When they get thrown therein, they hear its furor as it fumes." — *shahīqan* — the breath-intake of fire.

[67:8]: "It almost explodes from rage. Whenever a group is thrown therein, its guards would ask them, 'Did you not receive a warner?'"

[67:9]: "They would answer, 'Yes indeed; a warner did come to us, but we disbelieved and said, "GOD did not reveal anything. You are totally astray."'"

[67:10]: "They also say, 'If we heard or understood, we would not be among the dwellers of Hell!'" — *law kunnā nasma`u aw na`qilu mā kunnā fī aṣḥābi s-sa`īr*. The disbelievers' post-mortem self-diagnosis: *we did not listen, we did not reason*. The two faculties named are *sam`* (hearing) and *`aql* (reasoning) — the same two the Quran repeatedly identifies as the salvific channels.

**The believer's posture.**

[67:12]: "As for those who reverence their Lord, when alone in their privacy, they have attained forgiveness and a great recompense." — *yakhshawna rabbahum bi-l-ghayb*. The *invisibility-criterion* of reverence: reverence is *certified* by what one does *unseen*.

[67:13]–[67:14]: "Whether you keep your utterances secret, or declare them, He is fully aware of the innermost thoughts. Should He not know what He created? He is the Sublime, Most Cognizant." — the *epistemological argument*: *the One who created you, knows you*.

**The earth and the sky.**

[67:15]: "He is the One who put the Earth at your service. Roam its corners, and eat from His provisions." — *huwa lladhī ja`ala lakumu l-arḍa dhalūlā*. The earth is *subdued* / *made tractable* for human use.

[67:16]–[67:17]: "Have you guaranteed that the One in heaven will not strike the earth and cause it to tumble? Have you guaranteed that the One in heaven will not send upon you a violent storm? Will you then appreciate the value of My warning?" — *a-amintum man fī s-samā'*. Two *security-tests*: are you secure against earth-quaking and storm?

**The bird sign.**

[67:19]: "Have they not seen the birds above them lined up in columns and spreading their wings? The Most Gracious is the One who holds them in the air. He is Seer of all things." — the birds' aerodynamic suspension is offered as a *proof of God's holding*. *Mā yumsikuhunna illā r-Raḥmān* — "none holds them but the Most Gracious."

**The walking-on-the-face image.**

[67:22]: "Is one who walks while slumped over on his face better guided, or one who walks straight on the right path?" — *afa-man yamshī mukibban `alā wajhihī ahdā am-man yamshī sawiyyan `alā ṣirāṭin mustaqīm*. The image-pair: *face-down crawling* vs *upright walking on a straight path*.

[67:23]: "Say, 'He is the One who initiated you, and granted you the hearing, the eyes, and the brains. Rarely are you appreciative.'" — the three senses again.

**The death and the timing.**

[67:26]: "Say, 'Such knowledge is with GOD; I am no more than a manifest warner.'" — the Prophet's *limited authority*: he is *only* a warner; he does not know the timing.

[67:28]–[67:29]: "Say, 'Whether GOD decides to annihilate me and those with me, or to shower us with His mercy, who is there to protect the disbelievers from a painful retribution?'" — the Prophet's *humble framing*: *whatever God does to me, you are still under accountability*.

**The water test (67:30).**

[67:30]: "Say, 'What if your water sinks away, who will provide you with pure water?'" — *qul a-ra'aytum in aṣbaḥa mā'ukum ghawran fa-man ya'tīkum bi-mā'in ma`īn*. The closing rhetorical thrust: *if your water-table dropped, who would supply you*? An eco-theological closer.

**Synthesis.**

Kingship is God's ([67:1]); death-and-life are created as the *better-deed* test ([67:2]); the eye is invited to *look twice* for cracks in creation and finds none ([67:3]–[67:4]); the hell-dialogue identifies *not hearing, not reasoning* as the disbelievers' self-diagnosed failure ([67:10]); the believer is certified by what they do in *invisibility* ([67:12]); the earth is subdued for use, but the sky's strike is not guaranteed against ([67:15]–[67:17]); birds testify to God's holding ([67:19]); face-down crawling is the metaphor for the misguided life ([67:22]); the Prophet is *only* a warner ([67:26]); and the closing image is the water-table's potential withdrawal ([67:30]).

67:1.text: Most exalted is the One in whose hands is all kingship, and He is Omnipotent.
67:1.arabic: تبرك الذي بيده الملك وهو على كل شيء قدير

67:2.text: The One who created death and life for the purpose of distinguishing those among you who would do better. He is the Almighty, the Forgiving.
67:2.arabic: الذي خلق الموت والحيوة ليبلوكم أيكم أحسن عملا وهو العزيز الغفور

67:3.text: He created seven universes in layers. You do not see any imperfection in the creation by the Most Gracious. Keep looking; do you see any flaw?
67:3.arabic: الذي خلق سبع سموت طباقا ما ترى في خلق الرحمن من تفوت فارجع البصر هل ترى من فطور

67:4.text: Look again and again; your eyes will come back stumped and conquered.
67:4.arabic: ثم ارجع البصر كرتين ينقلب إليك البصر خاسئا وهو حسير

67:5.text: We adorned the lowest universe with lamps, and guarded its borders with projectiles against the devils; we prepared for them a retribution in Hell.
67:5.arabic: ولقد زينا السماء الدنيا بمصبيح وجعلنها رجوما للشيطين وأعتدنا لهم عذاب السعير

67:7.text: When they get thrown therein, they hear its furor as it fumes.
67:7.arabic: إذا ألقوا فيها سمعوا لها شهيقا وهي تفور

67:8.text: It almost explodes from rage. Whenever a group is thrown therein, its guards would ask them, "Did you not receive a warner?"
67:8.arabic: تكاد تميز من الغيظ كلما ألقي فيها فوج سألهم خزنتها ألم يأتكم نذير

67:9.text: They would answer, "Yes indeed; a warner did come to us, but we disbelieved and said, `GOD did not reveal anything. You are totally astray.' "
67:9.arabic: قالوا بلى قد جاءنا نذير فكذبنا وقلنا ما نزل الله من شيء إن أنتم إلا في ضلل كبير

67:10.text: They also say, "If we heard or understood, we would not be among the dwellers of Hell!"
67:10.arabic: وقالوا لو كنا نسمع أو نعقل ما كنا في أصحب السعير

67:13.text: Whether you keep your utterances secret, or declare them, He is fully aware of the innermost thoughts.
67:13.arabic: وأسروا قولكم أو اجهروا به إنه عليم بذات الصدور

67:14.text: Should He not know what He created? He is the Sublime, Most Cognizant.
67:14.arabic: ألا يعلم من خلق وهو اللطيف الخبير

67:15.text: He is the One who put the Earth at your service. Roam its corners, and eat from His provisions. To Him is the final summoning.
67:15.arabic: هو الذي جعل لكم الأرض ذلولا فامشوا في مناكبها وكلوا من رزقه وإليه النشور

67:16.text: Have you guaranteed that the One in heaven will not strike the earth and cause it to tumble?
67:16.arabic: ءأمنتم من في السماء أن يخسف بكم الأرض فإذا هي تمور

67:17.text: Have you guaranteed that the One in heaven will not send upon you a violent storm? Will you then appreciate the value of My warning?
67:17.arabic: أم أمنتم من في السماء أن يرسل عليكم حاصبا فستعلمون كيف نذير

67:19.text: Have they not seen the birds above them lined up in columns and spreading their wings? The Most Gracious is the One who holds them in the air. He is Seer of all things.
67:19.arabic: أولم يروا إلى الطير فوقهم صـفـت ويقبضن ما يمسكهن إلا الرحمن إنه بكل شيء بصير

67:22.text: Is one who walks while slumped over on his face better guided, or one who walks straight on the right path?
67:22.arabic: أفمن يمشي مكبا على وجهه أهدى أمن يمشي سويا على صرط مستقيم

67:23.text: Say, "He is the One who initiated you, and granted you the hearing, the eyes, and the brains. Rarely are you appreciative."
67:23.arabic: قل هو الذي أنشأكم وجعل لكم السمع والأبصر والأفـدة قليلا ما تشكرون

67:26.text: Say, "Such knowledge is with GOD; I am no more than a manifest warner."
67:26.arabic: قل إنما العلم عند الله وإنما أنا نذير مبين

67:28.text: Say, "Whether GOD decides to annihilate me and those with me, or to shower us with His mercy, who is there to protect the disbelievers from a painful retribution?"
67:28.arabic: قل أرءيتم إن أهلكني الله ومن معي أو رحمنا فمن يجير الكفرين من عذاب أليم

67:29.text: Say, "He is the Most Gracious; we believe in Him, and we trust in Him. You will surely find out who is really far astray."
67:29.arabic: قل هو الرحمن ءامنا به وعليه توكلنا فستعلمون من هو في ضلل مبين

67:30.text: Say, "What if your water sinks away, who will provide you with pure water?"
67:30.arabic: قل أرءيتم إن أصبح ماؤكم غورا فمن يأتيكم بماء معين

question: Explain Surah 60 (The Test) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 60 (Al-Mumtaḥanah — "The Test") is thirteen verses, Medinan. The surah is short, concrete, and unusually practical — it covers political-religious alliance, emotional ambivalence about kin in the disbelievers' camp, the Abrahamic model of *exemplary dis-affiliation*, the asylum protocol for women fleeing the disbelievers' camp, and the pledge-of-allegiance for new women converts.

**The opening — do not befriend the enemy.**

[60:1]: "O you who believe, you shall not befriend My enemies and your enemies, extending love and friendship to them, even though they have disbelieved in the truth that has come to you. They persecute the messenger, and you, just because you believe in GOD, your Lord. If you mobilize to struggle in My cause, seeking My blessings, how can you secretly love them? I am fully aware of everything you conceal, and everything you declare. Those among you who do this have indeed strayed off the right path." — the surah's anchor verse. *Lā tattakhidhū `aduwwī wa-`aduwwakum awliyā'a, tulqūna ilayhim bi-l-mawaddah* — "do not take My enemy and your enemy as allies, extending love to them."

The context, classically given, is Ḥāṭib ibn Abī Balta`ah's letter to the Quraysh disclosing Muhammad's preparations for the conquest of Mecca — a believing companion's *information leak* to the disbelievers' camp out of family-loyalty considerations.

**The reason given.**

[60:2]: "Whenever they encounter you, they treat you as enemies, and hurt you with their hands and tongues. They want you to disbelieve." — the empirical observation: *the disbelievers are not neutrals*.

[60:3]: "Your relatives and your money can never help you. On the Day of Resurrection, He will judge among you. GOD is Seer of everything you do." — *kin and money* cannot help you on the Day. Section header in Khalifa: "Abraham: An Example."

**The Abrahamic model.**

[60:4]: "A good example has been set for you by Abraham and those with him. They said to their people, 'We disown you and the idols that you worship besides GOD. We denounce you, and you will see nothing from us except animosity and hatred until you believe in GOD ALONE.' However, a mistake was committed by Abraham when he said to his father, 'I will pray for your forgiveness, but I possess no power to protect you from GOD.' 'Our Lord, we trust in You, and submit to You; to You is the final destiny.'" — *qad kānat lakum uswatun ḥasanatun fī Ibrāhīm*. Abraham is given as an *exemplar* of *dis-affiliation from the idolatrous community*.

The verse contains an extraordinary admission: Abraham's *prayer for his disbelieving father's forgiveness* is named, in Khalifa's translation, as *a mistake*. Classical exegesis softens this — but the Quranic text (cross-reference [9:114]) is clear that the prayer was *based on a promise made to his father*, which Abraham *withdrew once the father's enmity to God became clear*. Khalifa's rendering preserves the moral evaluation explicitly.

[60:5]: "Our Lord, let us not be oppressed by those who disbelieved, and forgive us. You are the Almighty, Most Wise." — Abraham's group's prayer continued.

[60:6]: "A good example has been set by them for those who seek GOD and the Last Day. As for those who turn away, GOD is in no need (of them), Most Praiseworthy." — the *uswah* (example) is restated.

**The mercy clause.**

[60:7]: "GOD may change the animosity between you and them into love. GOD is Omnipotent. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful." — `Asā llāhu an yaj`ala baynakum wa-bayna lladhīna `ādaytum minhum mawaddah*. The verse opens *the door of mercy*: *animosity* can be *converted to mutual love*. This conversion is in God's hand.

**The political-fairness clause.**

[60:8]: "GOD does not enjoin you from befriending those who do not fight you because of religion, and do not evict you from your homes. You may befriend them and be equitable towards them. GOD loves the equitable." — *lā yanhākumu llāhu `ani lladhīna lam yuqātilūkum fī d-dīn wa-lam yukhrijūkum min diyārikum an tabarrūhum wa-tuqsiṭū ilayhim*. The verse is one of the Quran's most-cited verses on *interfaith civility*: *peaceful disbelievers* may be befriended and *treated with equity*; *God loves the equitable* (*al-muqsiṭīn*).

[60:9]: "GOD enjoins you only from befriending those who fight you because of religion, evict you from your homes, and band together with others to banish you. You shall not befriend them. Those who befriend them are the transgressors." — the *prohibition* is specifically for the *combatant* disbelievers.

**The asylum protocol.**

[60:10]: "O you who believe, when believing women (abandon the enemy and) ask for asylum with you, you shall test them. GOD is fully aware of their belief. Once you establish that they are believers, you shall not return them to the disbelievers. They are not lawful to remain married to them, nor shall the disbelievers be allowed to marry them. Give back the dowries that the disbelievers have paid. You commit no error by marrying them, so long as you pay them their due dowries. Do not keep disbelieving wives (if they wish to join the enemy). You may ask them for the dowry you had paid, and they may ask for what they paid. This is GOD's rule; He rules among you. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise." — the *test* (whence the surah's title): asylum-seeking women are *tested*, their faith *verified*, and they are *not returned*. The dowry-economics are equalized in both directions.

[60:11]: "If any of your wives join the enemies' camp, and you are forced to fight, you shall force the enemy to compensate the men who lost their wives, by giving them what they spent on their wives. You shall reverence GOD, in whom you believe." — the reciprocal compensation rule.

**The women's pledge.**

[60:12]: "O you prophet, when the believing women (who abandoned the disbelievers) to seek asylum with you pledge to you that they will not set up any idols besides GOD, nor steal, nor commit adultery, nor kill their children, nor fabricate any falsehood, nor disobey your righteous orders, you shall accept their pledge, and pray to GOD to forgive them. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful." — the *bay`ah* of women: six commitments — no idolatry, no theft, no adultery, no killing children, no fabrication, no disobeying righteous orders.

[60:13]: "O you who believe, do not befriend people with whom GOD is angry, and who are hopelessly stuck in disbelief; they are just as hopeless as the disbelievers who are already in the graves." — closing refrain mirroring [60:1].

**Synthesis.**

Do not ally with combatant disbelievers ([60:1]–[60:2]); kin and money will not save you ([60:3]); Abraham models *exemplary dis-affiliation* — even, on the Khalifa reading, conceding Abraham's *one mistake* in praying for his idolatrous father ([60:4]); but God can *convert animosity to love* ([60:7]); meanwhile, *peaceful disbelievers* must be *befriended and treated equitably* ([60:8]) — *God loves the equitable*; the asylum protocol for fleeing women ([60:10]), the inter-camp dowry compensation ([60:11]), and the six-clause pledge for new women converts ([60:12]). The surah is *the test* — both of the men's allegiance and the women's faith.

60:1.text: O you who believe, you shall not befriend My enemies and your enemies, extending love and friendship to them, even though they have disbelieved in the truth that has come to you. They persecute the messenger, and you, just because you believe in GOD, your Lord. If you mobilize to struggle in My cause, seeking My blessings, how can you secretly love them? I am fully aware of everything you conceal, and everything you declare. Those among you who do this have indeed strayed off the right path.
60:1.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا لا تتخذوا عدوي وعدوكم أولياء تلقون إليهم بالمودة وقد كفروا بما جاءكم من الحق يخرجون الرسول وإياكم أن تؤمنوا بالله ربكم إن كنتم خرجتم جهدا في سبيلي وابتغاء مرضاتي تسرون إليهم بالمودة وأنا أعلم بما أخفيتم وما أعلنتم ومن يفعله منكم فقد ضل سواء السبيل

60:2.text: Whenever they encounter you, they treat you as enemies, and hurt you with their hands and tongues. They want you to disbelieve.
60:2.arabic: إن يثقفوكم يكونوا لكم أعداء ويبسطوا إليكم أيديهم وألسنتهم بالسوء وودوا لو تكفرون

60:3.text: Your relatives and your money can never help you. On the Day of Resurrection, He will judge among you. GOD is Seer of everything you do. Abraham: An Example
60:3.arabic: لن تنفعكم أرحامكم ولا أولدكم يوم القيمة يفصل بينكم والله بما تعملون بصير

60:4.text: A good example has been set for you by Abraham and those with him. They said to their people, "We disown you and the idols that you worship besides GOD. We denounce you, and you will see nothing from us except animosity and hatred until you believe in GOD ALONE." However, a mistake was commited by Abraham when he said to his father, "I will pray for your forgiveness, but I possess no power to protect you from GOD." "Our Lord, we trust in You, and submit to You; to You is the final destiny.
60:4.arabic: قد كانت لكم أسوة حسنة في إبرهيم والذين معه إذ قالوا لقومهم إنا برءـؤا منكم ومما تعبدون من دون الله كفرنا بكم وبدا بيننا وبينكم العدوة والبغضاء أبدا حتى تؤمنوا بالله وحده إلا قول إبرهيم لأبيه لأستغفرن لك وما أملك لك من الله من شيء ربنا عليك توكلنا وإليك أنبنا وإليك المصير

60:5.text: "Our Lord, let us not be oppressed by those who disbelieved, and forgive us. You are the Almighty, Most Wise."
60:5.arabic: ربنا لا تجعلنا فتنة للذين كفروا واغفر لنا ربنا إنك أنت العزيز الحكيم

60:6.text: A good example has been set by them for those who seek GOD and the Last Day. As for those who turn away, GOD is in no need (of them), Most Praiseworthy.
60:6.arabic: لقد كان لكم فيهم أسوة حسنة لمن كان يرجوا الله واليوم الأخر ومن يتول فإن الله هو الغني الحميد

60:7.text: GOD may change the animosity between you and them into love. GOD is Omnipotent. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful.
60:7.arabic: ۞عسى الله أن يجعل بينكم وبين الذين عاديتم منهم مودة والله قدير والله غفور رحيم

60:8.text: GOD does not enjoin you from befriending those who do not fight you because of religion, and do not evict you from your homes. You may befriend them and be equitable towards them. GOD loves the equitable.
60:8.arabic: لا ينهىكم الله عن الذين لم يقتلوكم في الدين ولم يخرجوكم من ديركم أن تبروهم وتقسطوا إليهم إن الله يحب المقسطين

60:9.text: GOD enjoins you only from befriending those who fight you because of religion, evict you from your homes, and band together with others to banish you. You shall not befriend them. Those who befriend them are the transgressors.
60:9.arabic: إنما ينهىكم الله عن الذين قتلوكم في الدين وأخرجوكم من ديركم وظهروا على إخراجكم أن تولوهم ومن يتولهم فأولـئك هم الظـلمون

60:10.text: O you who believe, when believing women (abandon the enemy and) ask for asylum with you, you shall test them. GOD is fully aware of their belief. Once you establish that they are believers, you shall not return them to the disbelievers. They are not lawful to remain married to them, nor shall the disbelievers be allowed to marry them. Give back the dowries that the disbelievers have paid. You commit no error by marrying them, so long as you pay them their due dowries. Do not keep disbelieving wives (if they wish to join the enemy). You may ask them for the dowry you had paid, and they may ask for what they paid. This is GOD's rule; He rules among you. GOD is Omniscient, Most Wise.
60:10.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا إذا جاءكم المؤمنت مهجرت فامتحنوهن الله أعلم بإيمنهن فإن علمتموهن مؤمنت فلا ترجعوهن إلى الكفار لا هن حل لهم ولا هم يحلون لهن وءاتوهم ما أنفقوا ولا جناح عليكم أن تنكحوهن إذا ءاتيتموهن أجورهن ولا تمسكوا بعصم الكوافر وسـلوا ما أنفقتم وليسـلوا ما أنفقوا ذلكم حكم الله يحكم بينكم والله عليم حكيم

60:11.text: If any of your wives join the enemies' camp, and you are forced to fight, you shall force the enemy to compensate the men who lost their wives, by giving them what they spent on their wives. You shall reverence GOD, in whom you believe.
60:11.arabic: وإن فاتكم شيء من أزوجكم إلى الكفار فعاقبتم فـاتوا الذين ذهبت أزوجهم مثل ما أنفقوا واتقوا الله الذي أنتم به مؤمنون

60:12.text: O you prophet, when the believing women (who abandoned the disbelievers) to seek asylum with you pledge to you that they will not set up any idols besides GOD, nor steal, nor commit adultery, nor kill their children, nor fabricate any falsehood, nor disobey your righteous orders, you shall accept their pledge, and pray to GOD to forgive them. GOD is Forgiver, Most Merciful.
60:12.arabic: يـأيها النبي إذا جاءك المؤمنت يبايعنك على أن لا يشركن بالله شيـا ولا يسرقن ولا يزنين ولا يقتلن أولدهن ولا يأتين ببهتن يفترينه بين أيديهن وأرجلهن ولا يعصينك في معروف فبايعهن واستغفر لهن الله إن الله غفور رحيم

60:13.text: O you who believe, do not befriend people with whom GOD is angry, and who are hopelessly stuck in disbelief; they are just as hopeless as the disbelievers who are already in the graves.
60:13.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا لا تتولوا قوما غضب الله عليهم قد يئسوا من الأخرة كما يئس الكفار من أصحب القبور

question: Explain Surah 72 (Jinns) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 72 (Al-Jinn — "Jinns") is twenty-eight verses, Meccan. The surah is unique in structure: most of its content is reported-speech *from the jinns themselves*, recounting what a group of them overheard of the Quran and concluded. The surah ends with a tight set of instructions to the Prophet about the limits of his knowledge and the integrity of the divine message.

**The opening — the jinn group's testimony.**

[72:1]: "Say, 'I was inspired that a group of jinns listened, then said, "We have heard a wonderful Quran."'" — *innā sami`nā qur'ānan `ajabā*. The jinns themselves describe the Quran as *`ajab* — wondrous, astonishing.

[72:2]: "'It guides to righteousness, and we have believed in it; we will never set up any idols beside our Lord.'" — *yahdī ilā r-rushd*. *Rushd* = right-direction, maturity. The jinns' first move on hearing is *belief* and *commitment to monotheism*.

[72:3]: "'The Most High is our only Lord. He never had a mate, nor a son.'" — the jinns' *creed* — *no mate, no son* for God.

**The Quran corrects jinn folk-theology.**

[72:4]: "'It is the foolish among us who used to utter such nonsense about GOD.'" — the jinns concede that *their own foolish* had previously fabricated theology.

[72:5]: "'We thought that neither the humans, nor the jinns, could possibly utter lies about GOD.'" — *the jinns thought lies about God were impossible*; their disabuse comes via the Quran.

[72:6]: "'Human beings used to seek power through jinn beings, but they only afflicted them with a lot of adversity.'" — *kāna rijālun mina l-insi ya`ūdhūna bi-rijālin mina l-jinn*. A direct refutation of jinn-magic / amulet practices: human seekers of jinn-power got *more adversity*, not less.

**The jinns' celestial intelligence-mission.**

[72:8]: "'We touched the heaven and found it filled with formidable guards and projectiles.'" — *mulit ḥarasan shadīdan wa-shuhubā*.

[72:9]: "'We used to sit there in order to spy. Anyone who listens is pursued by a powerful projectile.'" — the *meteor/shooting-star* phenomenon is given a *cosmic-security* reading: meteors are *projectiles against eavesdropping jinns*.

**The jinns' moral diversity.**

[72:11]: "'Some of us are righteous, and some are less than righteous; we follow various paths.'" — the jinns are *not monolithic*; they include both righteous and less-than-righteous. *Various paths* — *ṭarā'iq qidadā*.

**The conditional.**

[72:13]: "'When we heard the guidance, we believed therein. Anyone who believes in his Lord will never fear any injustice, nor any affliction.'" — *the believer is free of fear* of *bakhs* (injustice / reduction) and *rahaq* (overbearing).

[72:14]: "'Among us are the submitters, and among us are the compromisers.' As for those who submitted, they are on the right path." — *al-muslimūn* (submitters) and *al-qāsiṭūn* (compromisers / deviators) — two categories among the jinns paralleling humans.

[72:15]: "As for the compromisers, they will be fuel for Gehenna." — the *compromisers* get the same destiny as their human equivalents.

**The mosque-belongs-to-God instruction.**

[72:18]: "The places of worship belong to GOD; do not call on anyone else beside GOD." — *anna l-masājida li-llāhi fa-lā tad`ū ma`a llāhi aḥadā*. The verse is famously read by Khalifa as a *categorical prohibition* on *any* call on any other being in worship spaces — including saint-veneration or grave-visitation practices that became widespread in later Muslim folk religion.

**The Prophet's call and the limits of his power.**

[72:19]: "When GOD's servant advocated Him alone, almost all of them banded together to oppose him." — historical observation: monotheism *attracts collective opposition*.

[72:20]: "Say, 'I worship only my Lord; I never set up any idols beside Him.'" — *qul innamā ad`ū rabbī wa-lā ushriku bihī aḥadā*.

[72:21]: "Say, 'I possess no power to harm you, nor to guide you.'" — *qul innī lā amliku lakum ḍarran wa-lā rashadā*. The Prophet *explicitly disclaims* power-to-harm or power-to-guide. He *delivers*; he does not *cause salvation*.

[72:22]: "Say, 'No one can protect me from GOD, nor can I find any other refuge beside Him.'" — *the Prophet's own dependence* is identical to anyone else's.

[72:23]: "'I deliver GOD's proclamations and messages.' Those who disobey GOD and His messenger incur the fire of Hell, wherein they abide forever." — the Prophet's role: *delivery*. The accountability falls on the recipient.

**The unseen.**

[72:25]: "Say, 'I do not know if what is promised to you will happen soon, or if my Lord will delay it for awhile.'" — the *timing of the Day* is withheld from the Prophet.

[72:26]: "He is the Knower of the future; He does not reveal the future to anyone." — *`ālimu l-ghaybi fa-lā yuẓhiru `alā ghaybihi aḥadā*. The *general principle*: the future is *not revealed to anyone*.

[72:27]: "Only to a messenger that He chooses, does He reveal from the past and the future, specific news. He carefully guards the (revealed news) in front of him and behind him." — the *single exception* to [72:26]: a chosen messenger gets *specific* future-news, *guarded by escort angels*.

[72:28]: "This is to ascertain that they have delivered their Lord's messages. He is fully aware of what they have. He has counted the numbers of all things." — *li-ya`lama an qad ablaghū risālāti rabbihim, wa-aḥāṭa bi-mā ladayhim wa-aḥṣā kulla shay'in `adadā*. The closing verse — *He has counted the numbers of everything* — *aḥṣā kulla shay'in `adadā* — is one of Khalifa's foundational verses for the Code-19 mathematical-structure framework: God *counted everything by number*.

**Synthesis.**

A group of jinns overhears the Quran, believes immediately ([72:1]–[72:2]), corrects its own folk-theology ([72:4]–[72:5]), describes meteor-as-projectile cosmology ([72:8]–[72:9]), confesses moral diversity within its species ([72:11], [72:14]); the human Prophet is *only* a deliverer with no harm/guide power ([72:21]); the future is withheld from everyone except chosen messengers, and even then *escort-guarded* ([72:25]–[72:27]); and the closing verse claims *all things are counted by number* ([72:28]) — the Khalifa Code-19 grounding text.

72:1.text: Say, "I was inspired that a group of jinns listened, then said, `We have heard a wonderful Quran.
72:1.arabic: قل أوحي إلي أنه استمع نفر من الجن فقالوا إنا سمعنا قرءانا عجبا

72:2.text: " `It guides to righteousness, and we have believed in it; we will never set up any idols beside our Lord.
72:2.arabic: يهدي إلى الرشد فـامنا به ولن نشرك بربنا أحدا

72:3.text: " `The Most High is our only Lord. He never had a mate, nor a son.
72:3.arabic: وأنه تعلى جد ربنا ما اتخذ صحبة ولا ولدا

72:5.text: " `We thought that neither the humans, nor the jinns, could possibly utter lies about GOD.
72:5.arabic: وأنا ظننا أن لن تقول الإنس والجن على الله كذبا

72:6.text: " `Human beings used to seek power through jinn beings, but they only afflicted them with a lot of adversity.
72:6.arabic: وأنه كان رجال من الإنس يعوذون برجال من الجن فزادوهم رهقا

72:8.text: " `We touched the heaven and found it filled with formidable guards and projectiles.
72:8.arabic: وأنا لمسنا السماء فوجدنها ملئت حرسا شديدا وشهبا

72:9.text: " `We used to sit there in order to spy. Anyone who listens is pursued by a powerful projectile.
72:9.arabic: وأنا كنا نقعد منها مقعد للسمع فمن يستمع الأن يجد له شهابا رصدا

72:11.text: " `Some of us are righteous, and some are less than righteous; we follow various paths.
72:11.arabic: وأنا منا الصـلحون ومنا دون ذلك كنا طرائق قددا

72:13.text: " `When we heard the guidance, we believed therein. Anyone who believes in his Lord will never fear any injustice, nor any affliction.
72:13.arabic: وأنا لما سمعنا الهدى ءامنا به فمن يؤمن بربه فلا يخاف بخسا ولا رهقا

72:14.text: " `Among us are the submitters, and among us are the compromisers.' " As for those who submitted, they are on the right path.
72:14.arabic: وأنا منا المسلمون ومنا القسطون فمن أسلم فأولـئك تحروا رشدا

72:15.text: As for the compromisers, they will be fuel for Gehenna.

72:18.text: The places of worship belong to GOD; do not call on anyone else beside GOD.
72:18.arabic: وأن المسجد لله فلا تدعوا مع الله أحدا

72:20.text: Say, "I worship only my Lord; I never set up any idols beside Him."
72:20.arabic: قل إنما أدعوا ربي ولا أشرك به أحدا

72:21.text: Say, "I possess no power to harm you, nor to guide you."
72:21.arabic: قل إني لا أملك لكم ضرا ولا رشدا

72:22.text: Say, "No one can protect me from GOD, nor can I find any other refuge beside Him.
72:22.arabic: قل إني لن يجيرني من الله أحد ولن أجد من دونه ملتحدا

72:23.text: "I deliver GOD's proclamations and messages." Those who disobey GOD and His messenger incur the fire of Hell, wherein they abide forever.
72:23.arabic: إلا بلغا من الله ورسلته ومن يعص الله ورسوله فإن له نار جهنم خلدين فيها أبدا

72:25.text: Say, "I do not know if what is promised to you will happen soon, or if my Lord will delay it for awhile."
72:25.arabic: قل إن أدري أقريب ما توعدون أم يجعل له ربي أمدا

72:26.text: He is the Knower of the future; He does not reveal the future to anyone.
72:26.arabic: علم الغيب فلا يظهر على غيبه أحدا

72:27.text: Only to a messenger that He chooses, does He reveal from the past and the future, specific news.
72:27.arabic: إلا من ارتضى من رسول فإنه يسلك من بين يديه ومن خلفه رصدا

72:28.text: This is to ascertain that they have delivered their Lord's messages. He is fully aware of what they have. He has counted the numbers of all things.
72:28.arabic: ليعلم أن قد أبلغوا رسلت ربهم وأحاط بما لديهم وأحصى كل شيء عددا

question: What lessons does the Quran draw from the story of Sulaiman?
answer:
The Quran's Solomon-narrative is not just a story but a *teaching scaffold*. Several distinct lessons are drawn — by the text itself, by the characters within it, and by adjacent verses that comment on the episodes. The teaching cuts across gratitude, the relativization of power, the proper response to wealth, *not assuming the unseen*, and the integrity of prophets.

**1. Gratitude is the *first* response to extraordinary endowment.**

[27:15]: "We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, 'Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants.'" — *al-ḥamdu li-llāh*. Their *first action* on receiving knowledge is *thanks*. The verse is the foundational text for the lesson: *receipt of unique gift → immediate verbalized gratitude*, not boasting.

[27:16]: "Solomon was David's heir. He said, 'O people, we have been endowed with understanding the language of the birds, and all kinds of things have been bestowed upon us. This is indeed a real blessing.'" — Solomon *publicly identifies* his endowment as a *blessing*, not as a personal capacity. He gives the credit to its source.

**2. Power magnifies the obligation, not the freedom.**

[21:79]–[21:81]: David and Solomon's combined endowment — wisdom, knowledge, glorifying mountains, controlled wind — is bracketed by *we witnessed their judgment* in [21:78]. Power is exercised *under divine observation*.

[34:13]: "O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative." — *I`malū āla Dāwūd shukrā*. The family of David is *commanded to work in gratitude*. The very next clause notes how *few* are appreciative — power amplifies the test, and most fail it.

**3. The wealthy man's test passes through *refusal of the gift*.**

When the Queen of Sheba's envoys bring rich gifts hoping to forestall the conquest:

[27:36]: "What GOD has given me is far better than what He has given you." — Solomon refuses. The lesson: *the king who already has what he needs* refuses *the bribe disguised as diplomacy*. The Quran preserves this as a *moral counter-image* to the rulers who accept everything.

**4. Power tests by accumulation, not by deprivation.**

[38:34]: "We thus put Solomon to the test; we blessed him with vast material wealth, but he steadfastly submitted." — *fatannā Sulaymān*. The verse *names wealth as the test*. The test of David is the two-litigants (deprivation-of-justice); the test of Solomon is *vast material wealth*. The lesson: *tests come in both directions* — the rich are tested by *what they have*, not always by *what they lack*.

**5. Even prophets do not own the unseen.**

[27:18]–[27:19]: When the ant warns the colony of Solomon's army, Solomon *smiles*. The verse preserves his recognition that *a creature he commands has independent perception he did not have*. The seam between commanding nature and *fully knowing* it is *not closed*.

[34:14]: "When the appointed time for his death came, nothing indicated to them that he had died until an earthworm ate (his staff). When he fell down, the jinns realized that if they really knew the unseen, they would have stopped working for Solomon." — the *post-mortem disclosure*: the jinns *did not know* Solomon was dead until *a wormhole appeared in his staff and he fell*. The verse delivers an explicit lesson — *the jinns were not knowers of the unseen* — which the Quran then *exhibits* as a foundational anti-magic teaching.

**6. The accelerated retrieval — speed without arrogance.**

[27:40]: "One who possessed knowledge from the book said, 'I can bring it before you blink your eyes.' Then, when he saw it placed in front of him, he said, 'This is a blessing from my Lord, whereby He tests me, to show whether I am appreciative or unappreciative. Whoever is appreciative is appreciative for his own good, and if one turns unappreciative, then my Lord is in no need for him, Most Honorable.'" — the *blink-of-an-eye* retrieval of the throne is performed by *one with knowledge from the book*; Solomon's *response to receiving it* is *self-reflective gratitude*. His next utterance is a meditation on *his own being tested*.

**7. Solomon is defended against the post-mortem sorcery slander.**

[2:102]: "They pursued what the devils taught concerning Solomon's kingdom. Solomon, however, was not a disbeliever, but the devils were disbelievers." — the lesson is *meta-historical*: traditions that grew up *around* a prophet after his death cannot incriminate him. The Quran's defense of Solomon is also an instruction for how to read prophetic biographies skeptically.

**8. Knowledge of bird-language is *a real capacity*, not a metaphor.**

[27:16] and [27:18]–[27:19] are taken in the Quran *at face value* — Solomon hears the ant; he interrogates the hoopoe; he sends a letter via bird. The lesson: *some knowledge-capacities the Quran ascribes to past prophets are concrete*, not allegorical. (The Khalifa community generally preserves a literal reading here.)

**9. The throne-recognition test of the Queen — *appearances vs. structure*.**

[27:42]: Solomon disguises her throne to test her recognition; she says, "It seems that this is it." The lesson is *the queen's epistemological calibration*: she does not commit until she is sure. Wisdom in a sovereign includes *appropriate caution*.

[27:44]: She mistakes the crystal floor for a pool. Solomon corrects her. *She immediately submits* — *"My Lord, I have wronged my soul. I now submit with Solomon to GOD, Lord of the universe."* The lesson: *the truth-seeker's response to evidence is immediate submission*, not further negotiation.

**Synthesis of lessons.**

The Quran's Solomon teaches: *thank before you boast* ([27:15]–[27:16]); *power amplifies obligation* ([34:13]); *refuse bribes-disguised-as-gifts* ([27:36]); *wealth is the king's test* ([38:34]); *the unseen is not in your hand, even if you are a prophet* ([27:18], [34:14]); *speed is from God, not from you* ([27:40]); *prophets must be defended against post-mortem slanders* ([2:102]); *some capacities of the prophets are real, not symbolic* ([27:16]); and *the truth-seeker submits as soon as the evidence is clear* ([27:44]). The composite lesson is *the just-and-grateful sovereign*.

2:102.text: They pursued what the devils taught concerning Solomon's kingdom. Solomon, however, was not a disbeliever, but the devils were disbelievers. They taught the people sorcery, and that which was sent down through the two angels of Babel, Haroot and Maroot. These two did not divulge such knowledge without pointing out: "This is a test. You shall not abuse such knowledge." But the people used it in such evil schemes as the breaking up of marriages. They can never harm anyone against the will of GOD. They thus learn what hurts them, not what benefits them, and they know full well that whoever practices witchcraft will have no share in the Hereafter. Miserable indeed is what they sell their souls for, if they only knew.
2:102.arabic: واتبعوا ما تتلوا الشيطين على ملك سليمن وما كفر سليمن ولكن الشيطين كفروا يعلمون الناس السحر وما أنزل على الملكين ببابل هروت ومروت وما يعلمان من أحد حتى يقولا إنما نحن فتنة فلا تكفر فيتعلمون منهما ما يفرقون به بين المرء وزوجه وما هم بضارين به من أحد إلا بإذن الله ويتعلمون ما يضرهم ولا ينفعهم ولقد علموا لمن اشترىه ما له في الأخرة من خلق ولبئس ما شروا به أنفسهم لو كانوا يعلمون

21:79.text: We granted Solomon the correct understanding, though we endowed both of them with wisdom and knowledge. We committed the mountains to serve David in glorifying (God), as well as the birds. This is what we did.
21:79.arabic: ففهمنها سليمن وكلا ءاتينا حكما وعلما وسخرنا مع داود الجبال يسبحن والطير وكنا فعلين

21:81.text: For Solomon, we committed the wind gusting and blowing at his disposal. He could direct it as he wished, to whatever land he chose, and we blessed such land for him. We are fully aware of all things.
21:81.arabic: ولسليمن الريح عاصفة تجري بأمره إلى الأرض التي بركنا فيها وكنا بكل شيء علمين

27:15.text: We endowed David and Solomon with knowledge, and they said, "Praise GOD for blessing us more than many of His believing servants."
27:15.arabic: ولقد ءاتينا داود وسليمن علما وقالا الحمد لله الذي فضلنا على كثير من عباده المؤمنين

27:16.text: Solomon was David's heir. He said, "O people, we have been endowed with understanding the language of the birds, and all kinds of things have been bestowed upon us. This is indeed a real blessing."
27:16.arabic: وورث سليمن داود وقال يـأيها الناس علمنا منطق الطير وأوتينا من كل شيء إن هذا لهو الفضل المبين

27:18.text: When they approached the valley of the ants, one ant said, "O you ants, go into your homes, lest you get crushed by Solomon and his soldiers, without perceiving."
27:18.arabic: حتى إذا أتوا على واد النمل قالت نملة يـأيها النمل ادخلوا مسكنكم لا يحطمنكم سليمن وجنوده وهم لا يشعرون

27:19.text: He smiled and laughed at her statement, and said, "My Lord, direct me to be appreciative of the blessings You have bestowed upon me and my parents, and to do the righteous works that please You. Admit me by Your mercy into the company of Your righteous servants."
27:19.arabic: فتبسم ضاحكا من قولها وقال رب أوزعني أن أشكر نعمتك التي أنعمت علي وعلى ولدي وأن أعمل صلحا ترضىه وأدخلني برحمتك في عبادك الصـلحين

27:30.text: "It is from Solomon, and it is, `In the name of GOD, Most Gracious, Most Merciful.'
27:30.arabic: إنه من سليمن وإنه بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم

27:40.text: The one who possessed knowledge from the book said, "I can bring it to you in the blink of your eye." When he saw it settled in front of him, he said, "This is a blessing from my Lord, whereby He tests me, to show whether I am appreciative or unappreciative. Whoever is appreciative is appreciative for his own good, and if one turns unappreciative, then my Lord is in no need for him, Most Honorable."
27:40.arabic: قال الذي عنده علم من الكتب أنا ءاتيك به قبل أن يرتد إليك طرفك فلما رءاه مستقرا عنده قال هذا من فضل ربي ليبلوني ءأشكر أم أكفر ومن شكر فإنما يشكر لنفسه ومن كفر فإن ربي غني كريم

27:44.text: She was told, "Go inside the palace." When she saw its interior, she thought it was a pool of water, and she (pulled up her dress,) exposing her legs. He said, "This interior is now paved with crystal." She said, "My Lord, I have wronged my soul. I now submit with Solomon to GOD, Lord of the universe."
27:44.arabic: قيل لها ادخلي الصرح فلما رأته حسبته لجة وكشفت عن ساقيها قال إنه صرح ممرد من قوارير قالت رب إني ظلمت نفسي وأسلمت مع سليمن لله رب العلمين

34:12.text: To Solomon we committed the wind at his disposal, traveling one month coming and one month going. And we caused a spring of oil to gush out for him. Also, the jinns worked for him, by his Lord's leave. Any one of them who disregarded our commands, we subjected him to a severe retribution.
34:12.arabic: ولسليمن الريح غدوها شهر ورواحها شهر وأسلنا له عين القطر ومن الجن من يعمل بين يديه بإذن ربه ومن يزغ منهم عن أمرنا نذقه من عذاب السعير

34:13.text: They made for him anything he wanted - niches, statues, deep pools, and heavy cooking pots. O family of David, work (righteousness) to show your appreciation. Only a few of My servants are appreciative. Jinns' Knowledge is Limited
34:13.arabic: يعملون له ما يشاء من محريب وتمثيل وجفان كالجواب وقدور راسيت اعملوا ءال داود شكرا وقليل من عبادي الشكور

34:14.text: When the appointed time for his death came, they had no clue that he had died. Not until one of the animals tried to eat his staff, and he fell down, did the jinns realized that he was dead. They thus realized that if they really knew the unseen, they would have stopped working so hard as soon as he died.
34:14.arabic: فلما قضينا عليه الموت ما دلهم على موته إلا دابة الأرض تأكل منسأته فلما خر تبينت الجن أن لو كانوا يعلمون الغيب ما لبثوا في العذاب المهين

38:34.text: We thus put Solomon to the test; we blessed him with vast material wealth, but he steadfastly submitted.
38:34.arabic: ولقد فتنا سليمن وألقينا على كرسيه جسدا ثم أناب

38:35.text: He said, "My Lord, forgive me, and grant me a kingship never attained by anyone else. You are the Grantor."
38:35.arabic: قال رب اغفر لي وهب لي ملكا لا ينبغي لأحد من بعدي إنك أنت الوهاب

question: Explain Surah 44 (Smoke) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 44 (Ad-Dukhān — "Smoke") is fifty-nine verses, Meccan. The surah's title comes from the *profound smoke* prophesied in [44:10]. The surah is one of the *Ḥā Mīm* family (seven surahs opening with the letters *Ḥ. M.*) and one of the surahs Khalifa annotates with Code-19 letter-count analysis.

**The opening — the blessed night.**

[44:1]: "H. M." — *Ḥā Mīm*. The two-letter initial.

[44:2]: "And this enlightening scripture." — *wa-l-kitābi l-mubīn*. *Mubīn* = clarifying, making clear.

[44:3]: "We have sent it down in a blessed night, for we are to warn." — *innā anzalnāhu fī laylatin mubārakah*. The *blessed night* — classically identified with *Laylat al-Qadr* of surah 97. Khalifa preserves the identification.

[44:4]: "In it (the scripture), every matter of wisdom is clarified." — *fīhā yufraqu kullu amrin ḥakīm*. *Yufraq* — "is separated, distinguished" — every wise matter is *parted out* in the scripture.

[44:5]–[44:6]: "A predetermined command from us that we send messengers. This is a mercy from your Lord. He is the Hearer, the Omniscient." — sending messengers is a *raḥmah* (mercy) — the framing of revelation as God's compassionate act.

**The smoke prophecy.**

[44:9]: "Indeed, they are doubtful, heedless." — the audience's spiritual state.

[44:10]: "Therefore, watch for the day when the sky brings a profound smoke." — *fa-rtaqib yawma ta'tī s-samā'u bi-dukhānin mubīn*. The *day of clear smoke* — eschatological sign.

[44:11]: "It will envelope the people; this is a painful retribution." — *yaghshā n-nās*. The smoke *envelopes* the people.

[44:12]–[44:13]: "Our Lord, relieve this retribution for us; we are believers. Now that it is too late, they remember! An enlightening messenger had come to them."

[44:14]: "But they turned away from him, saying, 'Well educated, but crazy!'" — *mu`allamun majnūn*. The disbelievers' label-pair: *well-taught + crazy*.

[44:15]: "We will relieve the retribution for awhile; you will soon revert." — the temporary relief is *not* repentance-permanent; the disbelievers revert.

[44:16]: "The day we strike the big stroke, we will avenge." — *yawma nabṭishu l-baṭshata l-kubrā*. *Al-Baṭshatu l-Kubrā* — "the great strike" — a divine name for the Day.

**The Moses-Pharaoh narrative.**

[44:17]: "We have tested before them the people of Pharaoh; an honorable messenger went to them." — the *historical analogue*: the Pharaonic generation was *tested before* the current Meccan one.

[44:18]–[44:22]: Moses's call to Pharaoh, refusal to oppress, eventual prayer for relief: "These are wicked people."

[44:23]: "(God said,) 'Travel with My servants during the night; you will be pursued.'" — *fa-asri bi-`ibādī laylā*. The exodus command — depart by night.

[44:24]–[44:29]: The crossing of the sea, the drowning of Pharaoh's troops, the inheritance of their gardens / springs / crops / luxurious dwellings by *other people*, the cosmic indifference: "*Neither the heaven, nor the earth wept over them, and they were not respited*" ([44:29]).

[44:30]–[44:32]: The Israelites *saved from humiliating persecution* and *chosen knowingly* over all the peoples *of the time*.

**The disbelievers' question — resurrection.**

[44:34]–[44:36]: "The present generations say, 'We only die the first death; we will never be resurrected! Bring back our forefathers, if you are truthful.'" — the demand for *immediate empirical proof* — resurrect our fathers and we'll believe.

[44:37]: "Are they better than the people of Tubba` and others before them? We annihilated them for their crimes." — the *Tubba`* reference (the Himyaritic Yemeni dynasty) — they too had been *annihilated*.

**The purposive cosmos.**

[44:38]–[44:39]: "We did not create the heavens and the earth, and everything between them, just to play. We created them for a specific purpose, but most of them do not know." — *wa-mā khalaqnā s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍa wa-mā baynahumā lā`ibīn*. *Lā`ibīn* — "playing." Creation is *not play* — it has a *specific purpose* (*illā bi-l-ḥaqq*) most do not perceive.

**The Day of Decision.**

[44:40]: "The Day of Decision awaits them all." — *yawmu l-faṣl*.

[44:41]: "That is the day when no friend can help his friend in any way; no one can be helped." — the closure of *intercessory help* on the Day. *Mawlā* (friend / patron / ally) cannot help *mawlā*.

**The tree of bitterness — the food of the sinful.**

[44:43]–[44:46]: "Surely, the tree of bitterness will provide the food for the sinful. Like lye, it will boil in the stomachs. Like the boiling of hellish drinks." — *shajaratu z-zaqqūm* (the *zaqqum* tree) — the canonical hell-food, also in [37:62]–[37:65] and [56:52]. Its product *boils* in the stomach.

[44:47]–[44:50]: The sinner is *thrown into the center of hell*, scalding water poured on his head; he is mockingly told *"taste this; you were so powerful, so honorable."*

**The righteous in secure position.**

[44:51]: "The righteous will be in a secure position." — *inna l-muttaqīna fī maqāmin amīn*. *Maqām amīn* — *a secure position*.

[44:52]–[44:53]: Gardens, springs, *sundus* (fine silk) and *istabraq* (heavy brocade), reclining toward one another.

[44:54]: "We grant them wonderful spouses." — *wa-zawwajnāhum bi-ḥūrin `īn*. *Ḥūr `īn* — the classical *houris*. Khalifa renders *wonderful spouses* — emphasizing the *companion* aspect over the sensual.

[44:55]–[44:56]: All fruits, in *perfect peace*. *They do not taste death therein — beyond the first death* — *protected from hellfire*.

**The closing.**

[44:58]: "We have thus clarified it in your language, that they may take heed." — *fa-innamā yassarnāhu bi-lisānika*. *We have eased it in your tongue*. The Quran's *linguistic accessibility* in Arabic is named as God's intentional act of *yusr* (ease).

[44:59]: "Therefore, wait; they too will have to wait." — closing instruction to the Prophet — *patient waiting*.

**Synthesis.**

A blessed night, a clarifying scripture, the prophecy of *the great smoke* and the *great strike*, the historical analogue of Pharaoh ([44:17]–[44:33]), the disbelievers' resurrection-doubt ([44:34]–[44:37]), the *non-playful* purposiveness of creation ([44:38]–[44:39]), the Day of Decision ([44:40]), the *zaqqum*-tree as food of the sinful ([44:43]–[44:46]), the secure position of the righteous ([44:51]–[44:56]), and the *eased-into-your-tongue* clarification ([44:58]).

44:3.text: We have sent it down in a blessed night, for we are to warn.
44:3.arabic: إنا أنزلنه في ليلة مبركة إنا كنا منذرين

44:4.text: In it (the scripture), every matter of wisdom is clarified.

44:5.text: messengers. is a predetermined command from us that we send
44:5.arabic: أمرا من عندنا إنا كنا مرسلين

44:6.text: This is a mercy from your Lord. He is the Hearer, the Omniscient.
44:6.arabic: رحمة من ربك إنه هو السميع العليم

44:9.text: Indeed, they are doubtful, heedless. The Smoke: A Major Prophecy

44:10.text: Therefore, watch for the day when the sky brings a profound smoke.
44:10.arabic: فارتقب يوم تأتي السماء بدخان مبين

44:11.text: It will envelope the people; this is a painful retribution.

44:12.text: "Our Lord, relieve this retribution for us; we are believers."
44:12.arabic: ربنا اكشف عنا العذاب إنا مؤمنون

44:13.text: Now that it is too late, they remember! An enlightening messenger had come to them.
44:13.arabic: أنى لهم الذكرى وقد جاءهم رسول مبين

44:14.text: But they turned away from him, saying, "Well educated, but crazy!"
44:14.arabic: ثم تولوا عنه وقالوا معلم مجنون

44:15.text: We will relieve the retribution for awhile; you will soon revert.
44:15.arabic: إنا كاشفوا العذاب قليلا إنكم عائدون

44:16.text: The day we strike the big stroke, we will avenge.
44:16.arabic: يوم نبطش البطشة الكبرى إنا منتقمون

44:17.text: We have tested before them the people of Pharaoh; an honorable messenger went to them.
44:17.arabic: ۞ولقد فتنا قبلهم قوم فرعون وجاءهم رسول كريم

44:23.text: (God said,) "Travel with My servants during the night; you will be pursued.

44:30.text: Meanwhile, we saved the Children of Israel from the humiliating persecution.
44:30.arabic: ولقد نجينا بني إسرـءيل من العذاب المهين

44:38.text: We did not create the heavens and the earth, and everything between them, just to play.
44:38.arabic: وما خلقنا السموت والأرض وما بينهما لعبين

44:39.text: We created them for a specific purpose, but most of them do not know.
44:39.arabic: ما خلقنهما إلا بالحق ولكن أكثرهم لا يعلمون

44:40.text: The Day of Decision awaits them all.

44:41.text: That is the day when no friend can help his friend in any way; no one can be helped.
44:41.arabic: يوم لا يغني مولى عن مولى شيـا ولا هم ينصرون

44:51.text: The righteous will be in a secure position.

44:56.text: They do not taste death therein - beyond the first death - and He has spared them the retribution of Hell.
44:56.arabic: لا يذوقون فيها الموت إلا الموتة الأولى ووقىهم عذاب الجحيم

44:58.text: We have thus clarified it in your language, that they may take heed.

question: Explain Surah 63 (The Hypocrites) verse by verse.
answer:
Surah 63 (Al-Munāfiqūn — "The Hypocrites") is eleven verses, Medinan. Short, sharp, and historically specific — the surah responds to a particular hypocritical leader's threats during a military expedition (classically: `Abdullāh ibn Ubayy ibn Salūl, head of the Khazraj-aligned faction in Medina). It is one of the canonical *Friday surahs* — paired with surah 62, traditionally recited at Friday prayers.

**The opening — the false confession.**

[63:1]: "When the hypocrites come to you they say, 'We bear witness that you are the messenger of GOD.' GOD knows that you are His messenger, and GOD bears witness that the hypocrites are liars." — *idhā jā'aka l-munāfiqūna qālū nashhadu innaka la-rasūlu llāh*.
