The argument is *internal*: *you give yourselves sons but assign daughters to God* — *that's an unfair distribution*. (The verses are also famously associated with the *Satanic Verses* incident in some tradition; Khalifa preserves the standard reading without the contested interpolation.)

[53:23]: "These are but names that you made up." — *idol-names* are *human inventions*; God *never authorized* them. The argument is *theological*: *you cannot name your way into a god*.

**No intercession.**

[53:26]: "Not even the angels in heaven possess authority to intercede. The only ones permitted by GOD are those who act in accordance with His will and His approval." — *the intercession-cap*: even *angels in heaven* do not have intercessory authority *except* by God's permission.

[53:28]: "They had no knowledge about this; they only conjectured. Conjecture is no substitute for the truth." — *inna ẓ-ẓanna lā yughnī mina l-ḥaqqi shay'ā*. *Conjecture (ẓann) provides nothing of the truth*.

**The salvific minimum — no transferred burden.**

[53:32]: "They avoid gross sins and transgressions, except for minor offenses. Your Lord's forgiveness is immense." — *kabā'iru l-ithm* (great sins) avoided + *minor offenses* allowed under the *wide-open forgiveness*.

[53:36]: "Was he not informed of the teachings in the scripture of Moses?" — appeal to *prior revelation*.

[53:37]: "And Abraham who fulfilled?" — *fulfilled* what was commanded.

[53:38]: "No soul bears the sins of another soul." — *allā taziru wāziratun wizra ukhrā*. *No bearing soul bears another's burden* — this verse is repeated in *5 other places* in the Quran (6:164, 17:15, 35:18, 39:7) — one of the most-repeated theological claims.

[53:39]: "Every human being is responsible for his own works." — *wa-an laysa li-l-insāni illā mā sa`ā*. *Man gets only what he strove for*. The verse is the *core* of Quranic soteriology: *no inherited merit, no transferred guilt, no vicarious atonement*. Each person's account is *what they themselves did*.

**The signs continued.**

[53:43]–[53:49]: God makes laugh, makes cry, brings death, brings life, created the male and female, will effect re-creation, makes rich, makes poor, is Lord of *Sirius* (the star) — a striking specific astronomical name (*ash-shi`rā*) in the verse, which Khalifa often discusses.

**The historical roster.**

[53:50]–[53:54]: "He is the One who annihilated ancient `Aad. And wiped out Thamoud. Also the people of Noah before that. The evil communities (of Sodom and Gomorrah) were the lowliest. Consequently, they utterly vanished."

**The closing prostration.**

[53:62]: "You shall fall prostrate before GOD, and worship." — *fa-sjudū li-llāhi wa-`budū*. The closing imperative is the *prostration-verse* of surah 53 — one of the canonical *sajdah* moments. When the Prophet first recited this surah publicly before the Quraysh, *the whole audience prostrated involuntarily* — a moment preserved in early sīra.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Prophet's revelation is *not personal speech* but *seen inspiration* ([53:3]–[53:11]); the pre-Islamic female deities are *invented names with no authorization* ([53:19]–[53:23]); conjecture is no substitute for truth ([53:28]); *no soul bears another's burden*, and *each human gets only what he strove for* ([53:38]–[53:39]) — therefore *fall prostrate and worship* ([53:62]).

53:2.text: Your friend (Muhammad) was not astray, nor was he deceived.

53:3.text: Nor was he speaking out of a personal desire.

53:8.text: He drew nearer by moving down.

53:9.text: Until He became as close as possible.

53:10.text: He then revealed to His servant what was to be revealed.

53:11.text: The mind never made up what it saw.

53:13.text: He saw him in another descent.

53:17.text: The eyes did not waver, nor go blind.

53:18.text: He saw great signs of his Lord.
53:18.arabic: لقد رأى من ءايت ربه الكبرى

53:19.text: Compare this with the female idols Allaat and Al-`Uzzah.

53:21.text: Do you have sons, while He has these as daughters?

53:23.text: These are but names that you made up, you and your forefathers. GOD never authorized such a blasphemy. They follow conjecture, and personal desire, when the true guidance has come to them herein from their Lord.
53:23.arabic: إن هي إلا أسماء سميتموها أنتم وءاباؤكم ما أنزل الله بها من سلطن إن يتبعون إلا الظن وما تهوى الأنفس ولقد جاءهم من ربهم الهدى

53:26.text: Not even the angels in heaven possess authority to intercede. The only ones permitted by GOD are those who act in accordance with His will and His approval.
53:26.arabic: ۞وكم من ملك في السموت لا تغني شفعتهم شيـا إلا من بعد أن يأذن الله لمن يشاء ويرضى

53:28.text: They had no knowledge about this; they only conjectured. Conjecture is no substitute for the truth.
53:28.arabic: وما لهم به من علم إن يتبعون إلا الظن وإن الظن لا يغني من الحق شيـا

53:32.text: They avoid gross sins and transgressions, except for minor offenses. Your Lord's forgiveness is immense. He has been fully aware of you since He initiated you from the earth, and while you were embryos in your mothers' bellies. Therefore, do not exalt yourselves; He is fully aware of the righteous.
53:32.arabic: الذين يجتنبون كبـئر الإثم والفوحش إلا اللمم إن ربك وسع المغفرة هو أعلم بكم إذ أنشأكم من الأرض وإذ أنتم أجنة في بطون أمهتكم فلا تزكوا أنفسكم هو أعلم بمن اتقى

53:36.text: Was he not informed of the teachings in the scripture of Moses?
53:36.arabic: أم لم ينبأ بما في صحف موسى

53:38.text: No soul bears the sins of another soul.

53:39.text: Every human being is responsible for his own works.
53:39.arabic: وأن ليس للإنسن إلا ما سعى

53:43.text: He is the One who makes you laugh or cry.

53:50.text: He is the One who annihilated ancient `Aad.

53:62.text: You shall fall prostrate before GOD, and worship.

question: What is the central message of Surah 54 (The Moon)?
answer:
Surah 54 (Al-Qamar — "The Moon") is fifty-five verses, Meccan. The surah is one of the most structurally repetitive in the Quran — its center is built on a four-times repeated refrain: *wa-laqad yassarnā l-Qur'āna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakir* ("We have made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?"). The central message: *the warnings of past communities are still available, the Quran is *easy* to absorb, and the Day is closer than complacent disbelievers assume*.

**The opening — the moon split.**

[54:1]: "The Hour has come closer, and the moon has split." — *iqtarabati s-sā`atu wa-nshaqqa l-qamar*. *The Hour has come close, the moon split*. The verse is associated with the *moon-splitting miracle* tradition (when the Quraysh demanded a sign, the moon visibly split). Khalifa preserves this reading.

[54:2]: "Then they saw a miracle; but they turned away and said, 'Old magic.'" — *siḥrun mustamir*. *Continuous magic*. The disbelievers' deflection: even an unmistakable sign is dismissed as *the magician's standard trick*.

[54:3]: "They disbelieved, followed their opinions, and adhered to their old traditions." — *kullu amrin mustaqirr*. The disbelievers cling to their *fixed* prior commitments.

**The four-times refrain — Noah, `Aad, Thamoud, Lot.**

The surah's distinctive feature is four parallel narrative-blocks, each ending with the refrain *wa-laqad yassarnā l-Qur'āna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakir* — *We have made the Quran easy for recall; is there any who would take heed?*

**Block 1 — Noah ([54:9]–[54:17]).**

[54:9]: "The people of Noah disbelieved before them. They disbelieved our servant and said, 'Crazy!' He was persecuted."

[54:10]: "He implored his Lord, 'I am oppressed; grant me victory.'"

[54:11]: "We then opened the gates of the sky, pouring water." — *fa-fataḥnā abwāba s-samā'i bi-mā'in munhamir*.

[54:13]: "We carried him on a watercraft made of logs and ropes." — *dhāti alwāḥin wa-dusur*.

[54:15]: "We have set it up as a lesson. Does any of you wish to learn?"

[54:17]: "We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?" — first occurrence of the refrain.

**Block 2 — `Aad ([54:18]–[54:22]).**

[54:18]: "`Aad disbelieved. Consequently, how terrible was My retribution after the warnings."

[54:19]: "We sent upon them violent winds, on a day of continuous misery." — *rīḥan ṣarṣaran fī yawmin naḥsin mustamir*.

[54:22]: "We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?" — second.

**Block 3 — Thamoud ([54:23]–[54:32]).**

[54:23]–[54:31]: Thamoud's rejection, the camel-as-sign, the killing of the camel, the *one blast* (*ṣayḥatan wāḥidatan*) that left them like *harvested hay* (*hashīmi l-muḥtaẓir*).

[54:32]: "We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?" — third.

**Block 4 — Lot ([54:33]–[54:40]).**

[54:33]–[54:39]: The people of Lot's rejection, the showering with rocks (*ḥāṣiban*), the saving of Lot's family *at dawn* (*illā āla Lūṭin najjaynāhum bi-saḥar*), the warning ignored, the dawn-stroke (*ṣabbaḥahum bukratan `adhābun mustaqirr*).

[54:40]: "We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?" — fourth.

**Pharaoh — without the refrain.**

[54:41]–[54:42]: "Pharaoh's people were warned. They rejected all our signs. Consequently, we requited them as an Almighty, Omnipotent should."

**The closing argument — to the Prophet's audience.**

[54:43]: "Are your disbelievers better than those disbelievers? Have you been absolved by the scripture?" — *a-kuffārukum khayrun min ulā'ikum, am lakum barā'atun fī z-zubur*. *Is your generation any better than those past disbelievers? Do you have an exemption written down in some scripture?*

[54:45]: "All of them will be defeated; they will turn around and flee." — *sa-yuhzamu l-jam`u wa-yuwallūna d-dubur*. Tradition reports this verse predicted the Battle of Badr.

**The cosmic precision.**

[54:49]: "Everything we created is precisely measured." — *innā kulla shay'in khalaqnāhu bi-qadar*. The *qadar* (measure / decree) is *built into creation*.

[54:50]: "Our commands are done within the blink of an eye." — *wa-mā amrunā illā wāḥidatun ka-lamḥin bi-l-baṣar*. *One command, like the blink of an eye*.

**The closing — the position of honor.**

[54:54]: "Surely, the righteous have deserved gardens and rivers." — *inna l-muttaqīna fī jannātin wa-nahar*.

[54:55]: "In a position of honor, at an Omnipotent King." — *fī maq`adi ṣidqin `inda malīkin muqtadir*. *A seat of truth* (*maq`ad ṣidq*) *with an Omnipotent King* — the surah closes on the *destination* of the believer.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Hour is *near* and the moon-sign has already been given ([54:1]); the four standard examples (Noah, `Aad, Thamoud, Lot) all received warnings and all rejected and all were destroyed ([54:9]–[54:39]); the refrain *we made the Quran easy for recall* repeats four times — emphasizing that *the absorption is not the problem; the will is*; *your generation is no better than theirs* ([54:43]); and *everything is created in precise measure* ([54:49]), *every command is one and instantaneous* ([54:50]) — the righteous attain *a seat of truth with an Omnipotent King* ([54:55]).

54:1.text: The Hour has come closer, and the moon has split.

54:2.text: Then they saw a miracle; but they turned away and said, "Old magic."
54:2.arabic: وإن يروا ءاية يعرضوا ويقولوا سحر مستمر

54:3.text: They disbelieved, followed their opinions, and adhered to their old traditions.
54:3.arabic: وكذبوا واتبعوا أهواءهم وكل أمر مستقر

54:4.text: Sufficient warnings have been delivered to alert them.
54:4.arabic: ولقد جاءهم من الأنباء ما فيه مزدجر

54:5.text: Great wisdom; but all the warnings have been in vain.

54:9.text: The people of Noah disbelieved before them. They disbelieved our servant and said, "Crazy!" He was persecuted.
54:9.arabic: ۞كذبت قبلهم قوم نوح فكذبوا عبدنا وقالوا مجنون وازدجر

54:10.text: He implored his Lord, "I am oppressed; grant me victory."

54:11.text: We then opened the gates of the sky, pouring water.

54:13.text: We carried him on a watercraft made of logs and ropes.

54:15.text: We have set it up as a lesson. Does any of you wish to learn?
54:15.arabic: ولقد تركنها ءاية فهل من مدكر

54:17.text: We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?
54:17.arabic: ولقد يسرنا القرءان للذكر فهل من مدكر

54:18.text: `Aad disbelieved. Consequently, how terrible was My retribution after the warnings.
54:18.arabic: كذبت عاد فكيف كان عذابي ونذر

54:22.text: We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?
54:22.arabic: ولقد يسرنا القرءان للذكر فهل من مدكر

54:32.text: We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?
54:32.arabic: ولقد يسرنا القرءان للذكر فهل من مدكر

54:33.text: The people of Lot rejected the warnings.

54:40.text: We made the Quran easy to learn. Does any of you wish to learn?
54:40.arabic: ولقد يسرنا القرءان للذكر فهل من مدكر

54:49.text: Everything we created is precisely measured.

54:50.text: Our commands are done within the blink of an eye.
54:50.arabic: وما أمرنا إلا وحدة كلمح بالبصر

54:54.text: Surely, the righteous have deserved gardens and rivers.

54:55.text: In a position of honor, at an Omnipotent King.
54:55.arabic: في مقعد صدق عند مليك مقتدر

question: What is the central message of Surah 74 (The Hidden Secret)?
answer:
Surah 74 (Al-Muddaththir — "The Hidden Secret" / "The Cloaked") is fifty-six verses, Meccan. Khalifa identifies it as one of the *very first* revelations — traditionally the second after surah 96 (Al-`Alaq). The surah is also *the* foundational text in Khalifa's Code-19 framework: verse [74:30] reads simply *`alayhā tis`ata `ashar* — "over it is nineteen" — and verse [74:31] is the longest single verse of the Quran, explaining the function of the number nineteen as a sign for the believers, a test for the disbelievers, a confirmation for the People of the Book, and a removal of doubt.

**The opening — the cloak / hidden secret.**

[74:1]: "O you hidden secret." — *yā ayyuhā l-muddaththir*. *Muddaththir* — "the one wrapped in a cloak / mantle." Khalifa renders it as *hidden secret*, treating it as direct address to the Prophet (and, in some Khalifa-aligned readings, to the *messenger of the covenant* — Khalifa himself).

[74:2]: "Come out and warn." — *qum fa-andhir*. *Stand up and warn*. The verb-pair: *rise + announce warning*. This is the *original public-mission command* in Khalifa's tradition.

[74:3]–[74:7]: "Extol your Lord. Purify your garment. Forsake what is wrong. Be content with your lot. Steadfastly commemorate your Lord." — five staccato imperatives. *Praise / purify garment / forsake wrong (rijz) / no excessive giving in exchange for return / patient remembrance*. The *initial code* of the prophet's life.

**The Day's announcement.**

[74:8]–[74:10]: "Then, when the horn is blown. That will be a difficult day. For the disbelievers, not easy." — *yawmun `asīrun ghayru yasīr* — *a difficult day, not easy*.

**The case-study of the disbeliever.**

[74:11]–[74:25]: An extended portrait of a wealthy disbeliever (classically identified as *al-Walīd ibn al-Mughīrah*, the wealthy Meccan elder who, after listening to the Quran, returned and pronounced it *human-made magic*):

[74:11]: "Let Me deal with one I created as an individual." — *dharnī wa-man khalaqtu waḥīdā*.

[74:12]–[74:14]: God gave him *abundant wealth, children to behold, eased everything*.

[74:15]: "Yet, he is greedy for more." — *thumma yaṭma`u an azīd*. Even after the comfortable gift, he wants *more*.

[74:16]: "He stubbornly refused to accept these proofs." — *kāna li-āyātinā `anīdā*.

[74:18]: "For he reflected, then decided." — *innahū fakkara wa-qaddar*. *He thought and decided* — a *deliberate* refusal.

[74:19]–[74:20]: "Miserable is what he decided. Miserable indeed is what he decided." — repetition for emphasis.

[74:21]–[74:24]: He *looked*, *frowned*, *whined*, *turned away arrogantly*, then *said* — and the *said* is preserved in the next verses:

[74:24]: "He said, 'This is but clever magic!'" — *siḥrun yu'thar*. *Magic that has been handed down*.

[74:25]: "This is human made." — *in hādhā illā qawlu l-bashar*. *This is nothing but the speech of a human*. The very accusation the Quran is then refuting *by attaching the number-19 sign to it*.

**The 19-verses.**

[74:26]–[74:29]: "I will commit him to retribution. What retribution! Thorough and comprehensive. Obvious to all the people." — *saqar* (the second-level fire name) is introduced.

[74:30]: "Over it is nineteen." — *`alayhā tis`ata `ashar*. Five words; six syllables in the Arabic. The most-discussed single verse in Khalifa's Code-19 framework.

[74:31]: The longest verse: "We appointed angels to be guardians of Hell, and we assigned their number (19) (1) to disturb the disbelievers, (2) to convince the Christians and Jews (that this is a divine scripture), (3) to strengthen the faith of the faithful, (4) to remove all traces of doubt from the hearts of Christians, Jews, as well as the believers, and (5) to expose those who harbor doubt in their hearts, and the disbelievers; they will say, 'What did GOD mean by this allegory?' GOD thus sends astray whomever He wills, and guides whomever He wills. None knows the soldiers of your Lord except He. This is a reminder for the people."

The verse enumerates *five* explicit functions for the number 19: (1) disturbance of disbelievers, (2) conviction of Christians/Jews, (3) strengthening of believers, (4) removal of doubt, (5) exposure of doubters. The structure is *one of the rare points* where the Quran explicitly *names a function for one of its own numerical features*. Khalifa's *entire* Code-19 mathematical-miracle thesis is built on this verse — the number 19 is, on his reading, the *signal* he was sent to identify.

**The oaths and the warning.**

[74:32]–[74:34]: "Absolutely, (I swear) by the moon. And the night as it passes. And the morning as it shines." — three oaths.

[74:35]: "This is one of the great miracles." — *innahā la-iḥdā l-kubar*. *This* (the Quran, the number-19, the sign) is *one of the great miracles*.

[74:36]: "A warning to the human race." — *nadhīran li-l-bashar*.

[74:37]: "For those among you who wish to advance, or regress." — *li-man shā'a minkum an yataqaddama aw yata'akhkhar*. The freedom-clause.

**The accountability dialogue.**

[74:38]: "Every soul is trapped by its sins." — *kullu nafsin bi-mā kasabat rahīnah*. *Every soul is held in pledge for what it earned*. Then immediately the exception:

[74:39]: "Except for those on the right." — *illā aṣḥāba l-yamīn*.

[74:40]–[74:48]: In paradise, those-on-the-right *ask the guilty about the cause of their punishment*. The guilty answer in [74:43]–[74:46]:

[74:43]: "We did not observe the contact prayers (Salat)." — *lam naku mina l-muṣallīn*.

[74:44]: "We did not feed the poor." — *wa-lam naku nuṭ`imu l-miskīn*.

[74:45]: "We blundered with the blunderers." — *wa-kunnā nakhūḍu ma`a l-khā'iḍīn*. *We engaged in vain disputation with the disputers*.

[74:46]: "We disbelieved in the Day of Judgment." — *wa-kunnā nukadhdhibu bi-yawmi d-dīn*.

*Their own self-diagnosis*: four omissions and disbeliefs — *not praying, not feeding, idly debating, denying the Day*.

**The closing.**

[74:54]: "Indeed, this is a reminder."

[74:55]: "For whoever wills to take heed." — *fa-man shā'a dhakarah*. The freedom-clause again.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Prophet is told to *rise and warn*, beginning with *praise, purification, forsaking wrong, patient remembrance* ([74:1]–[74:7]); the wealthy disbeliever's *deliberate decision* to dismiss the Quran as *human speech* is named ([74:11]–[74:25]); the *number 19* is identified as the *guardian-of-hell* count with *five explicit functions* — the foundational text of Khalifa's Code-19 framework ([74:30]–[74:31]); every soul is *held in pledge* except those-on-the-right ([74:38]–[74:39]); the *self-diagnosis* of those in hell is *we did not pray, we did not feed the poor, we engaged in vain debate, we denied the Day* ([74:43]–[74:46]).

74:8.text: Then, when the horn is blown.

74:9.text: That will be a difficult day.

74:11.text: Let Me deal with one I created as an individual.

74:16.text: He stubbornly refused to accept these proofs.

74:20.text: Miserable indeed is what he decided.

74:24.text: He said, "This is but clever magic!
74:24.arabic: فقال إن هذا إلا سحر يؤثر

74:26.text: I will commit him to retribution.

74:31.text: We appointed angels to be guardians of Hell, and we assigned their number (19) (1) to disturb the disbelievers, (2) to convince the Christians and Jews (that this is a divine scripture), (3) to strengthen the faith of the faithful, (4) to remove all traces of doubt from the hearts of Christians, Jews, as well as the believers, and (5) to expose those who harbor doubt in their hearts, and the disbelievers; they will say, "What did GOD mean by this allegory?" GOD thus sends astray whomever He wills, and guides whomever He wills. None knows the soldiers of your Lord except He. This is a reminder for the people.
74:31.arabic: وما جعلنا أصحب النار إلا ملـئكة وما جعلنا عدتهم إلا فتنة للذين كفروا ليستيقن الذين أوتوا الكتب ويزداد الذين ءامنوا إيمنا ولا يرتاب الذين أوتوا الكتب والمؤمنون وليقول الذين في قلوبهم مرض والكفرون ماذا أراد الله بهذا مثلا كذلك يضل الله من يشاء ويهدي من يشاء وما يعلم جنود ربك إلا هو وما هي إلا ذكرى للبشر

74:38.text: Every soul is trapped by its sins.

74:39.text: Except for those on the right.

74:43.text: They will say, "We did not observe the contact prayers (Salat).

74:46.text: "We disbelieved in the Day of Judgment.

74:55.text: For those who wish to take heed.

question: What is the central message of Surah 66 (Prohibition)?
answer:
Surah 66 (At-Taḥrīm — "Prohibition") is twelve verses, Medinan. The surah is one of the Quran's most domestically situated passages — its first half addresses a household incident between the Prophet and two of his wives, and its second half offers four exemplary women as *types* of the believing and disbelieving wife.

**The opening — the prohibition.**

[66:1]: "O you prophet, why do you prohibit what GOD has made lawful for you, just to please your wives? GOD is Forgiver, Merciful." — *li-ma tuḥarrimu mā aḥalla llāhu laka*. *Why do you prohibit what God made lawful?* The opening rebuke is direct: the Prophet had *self-imposed* a prohibition (classical accounts vary: a prohibition on honey, or on intimacy with one wife to placate another). The Quran *rolls it back*: the Prophet *cannot prohibit what God has made lawful* — even to please family. The principle is one of the surah's most-cited: *household management is not a legislative tool*.

**The oath-resolution clause.**

[66:2]: "GOD has decreed for you the laws dealing with your oaths. GOD is your Lord, and He is the Omniscient, Most Wise." — *qad faraḍa llāhu lakum taḥillata aymānikum*. The *expiation framework* for oaths (cross-reference [5:89]) lets the Prophet break the self-imposed prohibition.

**The household incident.**

[66:3]: "The prophet had trusted some of his wives with a certain statement, then one of them spread it, and GOD let him know about it. He then informed his wife of part of the issue, and disregarded part. She asked him, 'Who informed you of this?' He said, 'I was informed by the Omniscient, Most Cognizant.'" — the incident is narrated *abstractly*; the wives' names are not in the Quranic text (classical tradition fills them in as Ḥafṣah and `Ā'ishah). The pattern: a confidence breached, divine disclosure, partial confrontation.

**The two-wife address.**

[66:4]: "If the two of you repent to GOD, then your hearts have listened. But if you band together against him, then GOD is his ally, and so is Gabriel and the righteous believers. Also, the angels are his helpers." — *in tatūbā ilā llāhi fa-qad ṣaghat qulūbukumā*. *If the two of you repent, your hearts have already deviated* — the Quran *names the alignment as already-deviated*, and *repentance is the corrective*. The escalation clause: *if you band together against him*, *God + Gabriel + the righteous believers + the angels* are all *the Prophet's allies*. The combination is unprecedented in the Quran for a domestic context.

[66:5]: "If he divorces you, his Lord will substitute other wives in your place who are better (Muslims), believers (Mu'mins), obedient, repentant, worshipers, pious, either previously married, or virgins." — the *substitution clause*: God can replace the Prophet's wives with *better* ones. The verse lists the *idealized wife-traits* in a sequence: *submitter, believer, obedient, repentant, worshiper, pious, previously-married or virgin*.

**The household-as-fire instruction.**

[66:6]: "O you who believe, protect yourselves and your families from the Hellfire whose fuel is people and rocks. Guarding it are stern and powerful angels who never disobey GOD; they do whatever they are commanded to do." — *qū anfusakum wa-ahlīkum nārā*. *Guard yourselves and your families from a fire*. The verse is one of the Quran's strongest statements on *familial religious responsibility* — protecting the *family* from hell-fire is *parallel to* protecting *the self*.

**The disbelievers' regret on the Day.**

[66:7]: "O you who disbelieved, do not apologize today. You are being requited only for what you did." — no post-judgment apologies accepted.

**The repentance command.**

[66:8]: "O you who believe, you shall repent to GOD a firm repentance. Your Lord will then remit your sins and admit you into gardens with flowing streams. On that day, GOD will not disappoint the prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will radiate in front of them and to their right. They will say, 'Our Lord, perfect our light for us, and forgive us; You are Omnipotent.'" — *tūbū ilā llāhi tawbatan naṣūḥā*. *Firm repentance* — the qualifier *naṣūḥ* (sincere, advising, not-to-be-returned-to). The believer's prayer on the Day: *perfect our light, forgive us*.

**The hard line on hypocrites and disbelievers.**

[66:9]: "O prophet, struggle against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be stern with them. Their abode is Gehenna, and a miserable destiny." — *jāhidi l-kuffāra wa-l-munāfiqīna wa-ghluẓ `alayhim*. *Struggle and be stern*.

**The four exemplary women.**

[66:10]: "GOD cites as examples of those who disbelieved the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were married to two of our righteous servants, but they betrayed them and, consequently, they could not help them at all against GOD. The two of them were told, 'Enter the Hell-fire with those who deserved it.'" — *imra'ata Nūḥin wa-imra'ata Lūṭ*. The *type* of *disbelieving wife of a believer*: *they could not help her*; she enters the fire *with the ones who deserved it*. The verse is one of the Quran's strongest *anti-vicarious-merit* claims: *prophetic marriage offers no salvation by association*.

[66:11]: "And GOD cites as an example of those who believed the wife of Pharaoh. She said, 'My Lord, build a home for me at You in Paradise, and save me from Pharaoh and his works; save me from the transgressing people.'" — *imra'ata fir`awn*. The wife of the *disbelieving tyrant* who *belied* and *believed*. Her prayer is for *a house with God* and *salvation from her own household*.

[66:12]: "Also Mary, the Amramite. She maintained her chastity, then we blew into her from our spirit. She believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures; she was obedient." — *Maryamu bnata `Imrān*. The closing example: Mary, *who maintained her chastity*, into whom *the spirit was breathed*, who *believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures*.

The four-woman structure: *two disbelieving wives of righteous men* (Noah's and Lot's wives) + *two righteous women* (Pharaoh's wife + Mary). The architectural symmetry: *marriage neither saves nor damns*; *the woman is a moral agent in her own right*.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Prophet cannot prohibit what God made lawful even to please his wives ([66:1]); the household incident discloses the principle — *spreading the messenger's confidences requires repentance* ([66:3]–[66:4]); the believer must *guard self and family from the fire* ([66:6]); the *firm repentance* (*tawbah naṣūḥah*) is the standing instruction ([66:8]); and the *four exemplary women* — two wives of righteous men who *betrayed*, two righteous women in unrighteous contexts who *prevailed* — testify that *no marital alliance saves or damns*; each soul is its own agent ([66:10]–[66:12]).

66:1.text: O you prophet, why do you prohibit what GOD has made lawful for you, just to please your wives? GOD is Forgiver, Merciful.
66:1.arabic: يـأيها النبي لم تحرم ما أحل الله لك تبتغي مرضات أزوجك والله غفور رحيم

66:2.text: GOD has decreed for you the laws dealing with your oaths. GOD is your Lord, and He is the Omniscient, Most Wise.
66:2.arabic: قد فرض الله لكم تحلة أيمنكم والله مولىكم وهو العليم الحكيم

66:3.text: The prophet had trusted some of his wives with a certain statement, then one of them spread it, and GOD let him know about it. He then informed his wife of part of the issue, and disregarded part. She asked him, "Who informed you of this?" He said, "I was informed by the Omniscient, Most Cognizant."
66:3.arabic: وإذ أسر النبي إلى بعض أزوجه حديثا فلما نبأت به وأظهره الله عليه عرف بعضه وأعرض عن بعض فلما نبأها به قالت من أنبأك هذا قال نبأني العليم الخبير

66:4.text: If the two of you repent to GOD, then your hearts have listened. But if you band together against him, then GOD is his ally, and so is Gabriel and the righteous believers. Also, the angels are his helpers.
66:4.arabic: إن تتوبا إلى الله فقد صغت قلوبكما وإن تظهرا عليه فإن الله هو مولىه وجبريل وصلح المؤمنين والملـئكة بعد ذلك ظهير

66:5.text: If he divorces you, his Lord will substitute other wives in your place who are better (Muslims), believers (Mu'mins), obedient, repentant, worshipers, pious, either previously married, or virgins. than you; submitters
66:5.arabic: عسى ربه إن طلقكن أن يبدله أزوجا خيرا منكن مسلمت مؤمنت قنتت تـئبت عبدت سـئحت ثيبت وأبكارا

66:6.text: O you who believe, protect yourselves and your families from the Hellfire whose fuel is people and rocks. Guarding it are stern and powerful angels who never disobey GOD; they do whatever they are commanded to do.
66:6.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا قوا أنفسكم وأهليكم نارا وقودها الناس والحجارة عليها ملـئكة غلاظ شداد لا يعصون الله ما أمرهم ويفعلون ما يؤمرون

66:8.text: O you who believe, you shall repent to GOD a firm repentance. Your Lord will then remit your sins and admit you into gardens with flowing streams. On that day, GOD will not disappoint the prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will radiate in front of them and to their right. They will say, "Our Lord, perfect our light for us, and forgive us; You are Omnipotent."
66:8.arabic: يـأيها الذين ءامنوا توبوا إلى الله توبة نصوحا عسى ربكم أن يكفر عنكم سيـاتكم ويدخلكم جنـت تجري من تحتها الأنهر يوم لا يخزي الله النبي والذين ءامنوا معه نورهم يسعى بين أيديهم وبأيمنهم يقولون ربنا أتمم لنا نورنا واغفر لنا إنك على كل شيء قدير

66:9.text: O prophet, struggle against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be stern with them. Their abode is Gehenna, and a miserable destiny.
66:9.arabic: يـأيها النبي جهد الكفار والمنفقين واغلظ عليهم ومأوىهم جهنم وبئس المصير

66:10.text: GOD cites as examples of those who disbelieved the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were married to two of our righteous servants, but they betrayed them and, consequently, they could not help them at all against GOD. The two of them were told, "Enter the Hell-fire with those who deserved it." Examples of the Believers: Pharaoh's Wife
66:10.arabic: ضرب الله مثلا للذين كفروا امرأت نوح وامرأت لوط كانتا تحت عبدين من عبادنا صلحين فخانتاهما فلم يغنيا عنهما من الله شيـا وقيل ادخلا النار مع الدـخلين

66:11.text: And GOD cites as an example of those who believed the wife of Pharaoh. She said, "My Lord, build a home for me at You in Paradise, and save me from Pharaoh and his works; save me from the transgressing people."
66:11.arabic: وضرب الله مثلا للذين ءامنوا امرأت فرعون إذ قالت رب ابن لي عندك بيتا في الجنة ونجني من فرعون وعمله ونجني من القوم الظـلمين

66:12.text: Also Mary, the Amramite. She maintained her chastity, then we blew into her from our spirit. She believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures; she was obedient.
66:12.arabic: ومريم ابنت عمرن التي أحصنت فرجها فنفخنا فيه من روحنا وصدقت بكلمت ربها وكتبه وكانت من القنتين

question: What is the central message of Surah 75 (Resurrection)?
answer:
Surah 75 (Al-Qiyāmah — "Resurrection") is forty verses, Meccan. The surah opens with a double-oath unusual in the Quran (a swearing-formula and a swearing-formula together), traverses a concise resurrection scene, includes one of the Quran's most explicit *meta-statements* about the Prophet's relation to the revelation, and closes with a *creation-as-resurrection-proof* argument.

**The double oath.**

[75:1]: "I swear by the Day of Resurrection." — *lā uqsimu bi-yawmi l-qiyāmah*. The construction *lā uqsimu* (literally "no, I swear" or "I do swear") is read by some classical grammarians as emphatic-affirmative (the *lā* is intensifying), by others as a refusal-to-need-to-swear (the matter is *too obvious to require oath*). Either reading, *the Day of Resurrection* is the first oath-referent.

[75:2]: "And I swear by the blaming soul." — *wa-lā uqsimu bi-n-nafsi l-lawwāmah*. *An-nafs al-lawwāmah* — *the self that blames itself* — is one of the Quran's three named *nafs-states*, alongside *an-nafs al-ammārah bi-s-sū'* ([12:53] — the soul commanding evil) and *an-nafs al-muṭma'innah* ([89:27] — the soul at peace). The *blaming soul* is the *intermediate* state — conscience active and self-critical.

**The resurrection question.**

[75:3]: "Does the human being think that we will not reconstruct his bones?" — *a-yaḥsabu l-insānu allan najma`a `iẓāmah*.

[75:4]: "Yes indeed; we are able to reconstruct his finger tip." — *qādirīna `alā an nusawwiya banānahū*. *Banān* — *fingertips*. The verse specifies *fingertip-level reconstruction* — and is sometimes cited (by Khalifa among others) as a *foreshadowing* of *fingerprint identification*: the *uniqueness* of human fingerprints as a sign of *resurrection-precision*. The classical reading is *we can even reconstitute his fingertips*; the Khalifa-aligned reading sees a *forensic precision* echo.

[75:5]: "But the human being tends to believe only what he sees in front of him." — *bal yurīdu l-insānu li-yafjura amāmah*. The human's epistemological habit: *evidence in his immediate sight*.

[75:6]: "He doubts the Day of Resurrection!" — *yas'alu ayyāna yawmu l-qiyāmah*. *When is the Day of Resurrection?* — *the question itself is the doubt*.

**The Day's arrival.**

[75:7]–[75:9]: "Once the vision is sharpened. And the moon is eclipsed. And the sun and the moon crash into one another." — *jumi`a sh-shamsu wa-l-qamar*. *The sun and the moon are gathered* — *combined*. The cosmic event.

[75:10]: "The human being will say on that day, 'Where is the escape?'" — *ayna l-mafarr*. *Where is the place of flight*?

[75:11]–[75:12]: "Absolutely, there is no escape. To your Lord, on that day, is the final destiny." — *kallā lā wazar* — *no refuge*.

**Self-judgment.**

[75:13]: "The human being will be informed, on that day, of everything he did to advance himself, and everything he did to regress himself." — *bi-mā qaddama wa-akhkhar*.

[75:14]: "The human being will be his own judge." — *bali l-insānu `alā nafsihī baṣīrah*. *The human is a clear-sighted witness against himself*. The verse closes any escape from self-knowledge: *you are the one watching yourself*.

[75:15]: "No excuses will be accepted." — *wa-law alqā ma`ādhīrah*. *Though he may offer his excuses*.

**The anti-Hadith / Quran-collection passage.**

Khalifa's translation places a section header before [75:16]: *"Muhammad Forbidden from Explaining the Quran"*. The four-verse block [75:16]–[75:19] reads:

[75:16]: "Do not move your tongue to hasten it." — *lā tuḥarrik bihī lisānaka li-ta`jala bih*.

[75:17]: "It is we who will collect it into Quran." — *inna `alaynā jam`ahū wa-qur'ānah*. *Collecting and Quran-ing* is *upon Us*.

[75:18]: "Once we recite it, you shall follow such a Quran." — *fa-idhā qara'nāhu fa-ttabi` qur'ānah*. *When we recite it, follow its recitation*.

[75:19]: "Then it is we who will explain it." — *thumma inna `alaynā bayānah*. *Its explanation is upon Us*.

For Khalifa, this four-verse block is a *foundational anti-Hadith text*: the *collection*, the *recitation*, and the *explanation* of the Quran are *all four operations* assigned to God Himself — not to the Prophet, not to the community, not to later commentators. Therefore (in Khalifa's reading): the Quran is *self-explanatory through divine arrangement*; no extra-Quranic *bayān* (explanation) is authorized.

**The diagnosis.**

[75:20]–[75:21]: "Indeed, you love this fleeting life. While disregarding the Hereafter." — *bal tuḥibbūna l-`ājilah, wa-tadharūna l-ākhirah*. *You love the immediate, you abandon the latter*. The diagnosis of preference.

**The two-face panel.**

[75:22]: "Some faces, on that day, will be happy."

[75:23]: "Looking at their Lord." — *ilā rabbihā nāẓirah*. The classical *vision-of-God* verse — a contested verse in Islamic theology. Khalifa preserves the literal rendering.

[75:24]–[75:25]: Other faces miserable, expecting the worst.

**The death-moment.**

[75:26]–[75:30]: "Indeed, when (the soul) reaches the throat. And it is ordered: 'Let go!' He knows it is the end. Each leg will lay motionless next to the other leg. To your Lord, on that day, is the summoning." — *idhā balaghati t-tarāqī*. The soul reaches the *collarbones* — the dying moment.

**The post-mortem indictment.**

[75:31]: "For he observed neither the charity, nor the contact prayers (Salat)." — *fa-lā ṣaddaqa wa-lā ṣallā*. Two specific omissions: *did not give in charity, did not pray*.

[75:32]: "But he disbelieved and turned away."

**The closing argument — creation-as-resurrection-proof.**

[75:36]: "Does the human being think that he will go to nothing?" — *ayaḥsabu l-insānu an yutraka sudā*. *Does he think he will be left aimless / untended?*

[75:37]: "Was he not a drop of ejected semen?" — *alam yaku nuṭfatan min maniyyin yumnā*. The biological reminder.

[75:38]: "Then He created an embryo out of it!" — *thumma kāna `alaqatan fa-khalaqa fa-sawwā*. *Embryo, then he created and shaped*.

[75:39]: "He made it into male or female!" — *fa-ja`ala minhu z-zawjayni dh-dhakara wa-l-unthā*.

[75:40]: "Is He then unable to revive the dead?" — *a-laysa dhālika bi-qādirin `alā an yuḥyiya l-mawtā*. The closing rhetorical question: *if He made you from a drop, can He not revive you?*

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Day of Resurrection is *imminent enough to be sworn upon* ([75:1]); the soul-that-blames-itself is the *intermediate moral instrument* ([75:2]); resurrection-precision extends to *the fingertip* ([75:4]); the human is *his own clear-sighted witness against himself* on that Day ([75:14]); the Quran's *collection*, *recitation*, and *explanation* are *all assigned to God Himself* — therefore the Quran is self-explanatory ([75:16]–[75:19]); preference for the *immediate* over the *latter* is the diagnosis ([75:20]); and the *creation-from-a-drop* argument *guarantees the resurrection* ([75:37]–[75:40]).

75:1.text: I swear by the Day of Resurrection.

75:2.text: And I swear by the blaming soul.

75:3.text: Does the human being think that we will not reconstruct his bones?

75:4.text: Yes indeed; we are able to reconstruct his finger tip.
75:4.arabic: بلى قدرين على أن نسوي بنانه

75:5.text: But the human being tends to believe only what he sees in front of him.

75:6.text: He doubts the Day of Resurrection!

75:9.text: And the sun and the moon crash into one another.

75:10.text: The human being will say on that day, "Where is the escape?"

75:12.text: To your Lord, on that day, is the final destiny.

75:13.text: The human being will be informed, on that day, of everything he did to advance himself, and everything he did to regress himself.
75:13.arabic: ينبؤا الإنسن يومئذ بما قدم وأخر

75:14.text: The human being will be his own judge.

75:15.text: No excuses will be accepted. Muhammad Forbidden from Explaining the Quran

75:16.text: Do not move your tongue to hasten it.
75:16.arabic: لا تحرك به لسانك لتعجل به

75:17.text: It is we who will collect it into Quran.

75:18.text: Once we recite it, you shall follow such a Quran.

75:19.text: Then it is we who will explain it.

75:20.text: Indeed, you love this fleeting life.

75:22.text: Some faces, on that day, will be happy.

75:26.text: Indeed, when (the soul) reaches the throat.

75:29.text: Each leg will lay motionless next to the other leg.

75:30.text: To your Lord, on that day, is the summoning.

75:31.text: For he observed neither the charity, nor the contact prayers (Salat).

75:32.text: But he disbelieved and turned away.

75:36.text: Does the human being think that he will go to nothing?

75:37.text: Was he not a drop of ejected semen?
75:37.arabic: ألم يك نطفة من مني يمنى

75:38.text: Then He created an embryo out of it!

75:39.text: He made it into male or female!

75:40.text: Is He then unable to revive the dead?
75:40.arabic: أليس ذلك بقدر على أن يحـي الموتى

question: What are the mysterious letters at the start of Surah 44 and what does the Quran do with them?
answer:
Surah 44 (Ad-Dukhān — "Smoke") opens with the two-letter initial *Ḥā Mīm* ([44:1]) followed immediately by an oath on the scripture: *and this enlightening scripture* ([44:2]). These two letters, *Ḥ. M.*, are one of the Quran's *muqaṭṭa`āt* (literally "the disconnected letters") — the most-studied unsolved riddle of the Quran's surface.

**1. What the muqaṭṭa`āt are.**

Twenty-nine surahs open with one or more isolated Arabic letters, recited individually (not as a word). Across these 29 openings, only 14 distinct letters are used — exactly *half* the Arabic alphabet's 28-letter inventory. Forms include single letters (e.g., *Qāf* in [50:1], *Nūn* in [68:1], *Ṣād* in [38:1]), pairs (*Ḥā Mīm* in [40:1], [41:1], [43:1], [44:1], [45:1], [46:1]; *Ṭā Hā* in [20:1]; *Yā Sīn* in [36:1]), triples (*Alif Lām Mīm* in [2:1], [3:1], [29:1], [30:1], [31:1], [32:1]; *Alif Lām Rā* in surahs 10–15), and longer combinations (*Kāf Hā Yā `Ayn Ṣād* in [19:1]; *Ḥā Mīm `Ayn Sīn Qāf* in [42:1]–[42:2]). The seven *Ḥā Mīm* surahs (40–46) form the most cohesive group.

**2. The classical position.**

The dominant classical view: *Allāhu a`lam bi-murādih* — "God knows best what He intended." The letters are treated as one of the Quran's *mutashābihāt* (passages whose definitive interpretation is reserved). Several proposed exegeses exist (abbreviations of divine names, mnemonic devices, *challenge markers* — *here is your alphabet, now produce a sūra like it*), but no single explanation has carried the field.

**3. Khalifa's Code-19 framework.**

The Khalifa community (in which the present knowledge graph operates) holds a specific reading: the muqaṭṭa`āt are the *signature* of the Quran's *mathematical structure*, and their function is precisely the one the Quran *self-explains* at [74:30]–[74:31] — to be a *sign* that *disturbs the disbelievers, convinces scripture-people, strengthens the faithful, removes doubt, and exposes doubters*.

The operational claim on which the framework rests: in each muqaṭṭa`āt-initialed surah, *the frequency of the initial letters within the surah's text* is a *multiple of 19* — and *no closer prime captures the same data*. Khalifa worked through the cases:

- *Qāf*: surahs 50 (initialed with *Qāf*) and 42 (initialed with *Ḥā Mīm `Ayn Sīn Qāf*) contain *equal* counts of the letter *Qāf*: 57 = 19 × 3 in each. Combined, 114 = 19 × 6 (and 114 is the surah-count of the Quran).
- *Nūn*: surah 68 (the only *Nūn*-initialed) contains the letter *Nūn* 133 times = 19 × 7.
- *Ṣād*: across the three *Ṣād*-initialed surahs (7, 19, 38), the total *Ṣād* count is 152 = 19 × 8.
- *Ḥā Mīm*: the cumulative letter-frequency of *Ḥ* and *M* across the seven Ḥā Mīm surahs (40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46) is divisible by 19.

**4. Surah 44's specific case.**

[44:1] reads *Ḥā Mīm*. The two letters in this surah's text (combined frequencies of *ḥ* and *m*) participate in the cumulative *Ḥā Mīm* statistic across the seven-surah group. The verse [44:2] then identifies the immediate function: *and this enlightening scripture* — the muqaṭṭa`āt are not independent of the surrounding scripture; they preface a *book-claim*. The pattern is general: muqaṭṭa`āt are followed almost always by *a reference to the scripture itself* (cf. [2:2] "this is the scripture, no doubt"; [50:1] "Q., and the glorious Quran"; [38:1] "Ṣ., and the Quran containing the reminder").

**5. What the Quran does with them in surah 44.**

The structural function in surah 44:

(a) The letters serve as a *prologue marker* — the audience hears *Ḥā Mīm* and knows a particular *kind* of sūra follows.

(b) They preface the surah's *book-claim* ([44:2]: *wa-l-kitābi l-mubīn*).

(c) They preface the *blessed night* claim ([44:3]) — placing the revelation in *time* (Laylat al-Qadr).

(d) Their frequency within the surah participates in the larger *Ḥ-M* counting structure across the seven Ḥā Mīm surahs ([40:1], [41:1], [42:1], [43:1], [44:1], [45:1], [46:1]).

(e) The verse [74:31] explicitly assigns *five functions* to the Quran's numerical structure: disturbing disbelievers, convincing People of the Book, strengthening the believers, removing doubt, exposing doubters. The muqaṭṭa`āt are, on Khalifa's reading, the *most-visible terminals* of that structure.

**Synthesis.**

The two letters *Ḥā Mīm* at [44:1] are one of the Quran's twenty-nine *muqaṭṭa`āt* openings. The classical position withholds definitive interpretation; the Khalifa-aligned reading identifies them as the *signature* of the Quran's *Code-19 mathematical structure*, with the function self-explained at [74:30]–[74:31]. In surah 44 specifically, they preface the *book-claim*, the *blessed-night claim*, and participate in the seven-surah *Ḥā Mīm* counting pattern.

68:1.text: NuN, the pen, and what they (the people) write.

74:31.text: We appointed angels to be guardians of Hell, and we assigned their number (19) (1) to disturb the disbelievers, (2) to convince the Christians and Jews (that this is a divine scripture), (3) to strengthen the faith of the faithful, (4) to remove all traces of doubt from the hearts of Christians, Jews, as well as the believers, and (5) to expose those who harbor doubt in their hearts, and the disbelievers; they will say, "What did GOD mean by this allegory?" GOD thus sends astray whomever He wills, and guides whomever He wills. None knows the soldiers of your Lord except He. This is a reminder for the people.
74:31.arabic: وما جعلنا أصحب النار إلا ملـئكة وما جعلنا عدتهم إلا فتنة للذين كفروا ليستيقن الذين أوتوا الكتب ويزداد الذين ءامنوا إيمنا ولا يرتاب الذين أوتوا الكتب والمؤمنون وليقول الذين في قلوبهم مرض والكفرون ماذا أراد الله بهذا مثلا كذلك يضل الله من يشاء ويهدي من يشاء وما يعلم جنود ربك إلا هو وما هي إلا ذكرى للبشر

question: What is the central message of Surah 70 (The Heights)?
answer:
Surah 70 (Al-Ma`ārij — "The Heights" / "The Ascending Stairways") is forty-four verses, Meccan. The surah opens with a disbeliever *demanding the retribution* he doubts will arrive, then immediately *describes the Day's structure* (the angelic ascent on stairways), then *defines the believers* operationally, and *closes* with a warning of imminent arrival.

**The opening — the demand for retribution.**

[70:1]: "A questioner may question the inevitable retribution." — *sa'ala sā'ilun bi-`adhābin wāqi`*. *A questioner asked about a retribution to fall*. The verse imagines a specific disbeliever *demanding* the retribution they assume *will not* come.

[70:2]: "For the disbelievers, none can stop it." — *li-l-kāfirīna laysa lahū dāfi`*. Whether or not the disbeliever invites the retribution, *no one can stop it*.

[70:3]: "From GOD; Possessor of the highest Height." — *dhī l-ma`ārij*. *Ma`ārij* (plural of *mi`rāj*) — *ascending stairways, ladders*. The verse gives the surah its title and points to *upward channels*.

**The fifty-thousand-year day.**

[70:4]: "The angels, with their reports, climb to Him in a day that equals fifty thousand years." — *ta`ruju l-malā'ikatu wa-r-rūḥu ilayhi fī yawmin kāna miqdāruhū khamsīna alfa sanah*. *Angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day that is fifty thousand years*. The verse offers a *time-scale* statement contrasting with [32:5]'s *thousand-year day*; classical exegesis treats them as different time-units for different ascents; Khalifa's framework treats them as different *reference frames*.

**The instruction to patient waiting.**

[70:5]: "Therefore, you shall resort to a gracious patience." — *fa-ṣbir ṣabran jamīlā*. *Beautiful patience* — patience without complaint to anyone but God.

[70:6]: "For they see it far away." — *innahum yarawnahū ba`īdā*. The disbelievers see the Day as *far*.

[70:7]: "While we see it very close." — *wa-narāhu qarībā*. God sees it *close*. The verse-pair sets a *perception gap*: the disbelievers' time-sense and God's time-sense.

**The day's events.**

[70:8]–[70:14]: The sky like molten metal, the mountains like wool, no friend asks his friend — the guilty would *ransom* themselves from the retribution with their *children, spouse, brother, kin who sheltered them, everyone on earth* — and *none of it* would save them.

**The clay-fire image.**

[70:15]–[70:18]: "Indeed, the retribution is awesome. Eager to roast. It calls on those who turned away. Those who hoarded and counted." — *innahā laẓā* — *it is a blazing fire*. *Eager to roast* — *nazzā`atan li-sh-shawā*. The fire's *eagerness* is rendered as the verb of an *active agent*. The indictment: *those who hoarded and counted (wealth)*. The hoarder-counter is the standing target of this surah.

**The human's structural anxiety.**

[70:19]: "Indeed, the human being is anxious." — *inna l-insāna khuliqa halū`ā*. *Halū`* — *anxious, restless, frantic*. The verse is the surah's *anthropological diagnosis*.

[70:20]: "When adversity touches him, he is miserable."

[70:21]: "When blessed with resources, he is stingy." — the *two-sided* failure of the anxious human: *despair* when reduced, *stinginess* when comfortable.

**The exception — the believers.**

[70:22]: "Except for the worshipers."

[70:23]: "Who always observe their contact prayers (Salat)." — *alladhīna hum `alā ṣalātihim dā'imūn*. *Permanent* in their prayers. *Dā'im* — *standing, continuous*.

[70:24]: "Part of their money is set aside."

[70:25]: "For the poor and the needy." — *ḥaqqun ma`lūm li-s-sā'ili wa-l-maḥrūm*. *A known right for the asker and the deprived*. Compare [51:19]: same operational signature — *a known share of wealth for the asker and the deprived*.

[70:26]: "They believe in the Day of Judgment."

[70:27]: "They reverence their Lord's requital." — *yakhshawna `adhāba rabbihim*.

[70:28]–[70:31]: They guard their chastity except with their spouses; they honor their trusts and pledges; they bear truthful witness; *they observe their contact prayers*. The *operational profile of the believer* in one paragraph.

**The closing — the imminent day.**

[70:32]–[70:34]: trust-honoring, witness-bearing, prayer-observing — the same operational profile *bracketed* by an inclusio.

[70:35]: "These have deserved a place of honor in Paradise." — *ulā'ika fī jannātin mukramūn*.

[70:42]–[70:44]: closing — *disregard them until they meet the day they were promised* — *the day they will emerge from the graves, hurriedly, as if rushing to a banner* — *with their eyes humiliated, covered in shame*.

**Central message in one sentence.**

A questioner mocks-asks about the retribution he assumes won't come ([70:1]); the angels ascend to God on stairways in a fifty-thousand-year day ([70:4]); *the human is structurally anxious* — despairing in adversity, stingy in comfort ([70:19]–[70:21]) — *except* the believer-class, defined operationally as *permanent in prayer, known-share-for-the-asker-and-deprived, Day-believer, requital-reverer, chastity-guard, trust-honorer, truth-witness* ([70:22]–[70:34]) — these *have deserved a place of honor* ([70:35]); the day is *closer than they think* ([70:7]).

70:1.text: A questioner may question the inevitable retribution.

70:2.text: For the disbelievers, none can stop it.

70:3.text: From GOD; Possessor of the highest Height.

70:4.text: The angels, with their reports, climb to Him in a day that equals fifty thousand years.
70:4.arabic: تعرج الملـئكة والروح إليه في يوم كان مقداره خمسين ألف سنة

70:5.text: Therefore, you shall resort to a gracious patience.

70:6.text: For they see it far away.

70:7.text: While we see it very close.

70:8.text: The day will come when the sky will be like molten rocks.

70:11.text: When they see them, the guilty will wish he could give his own children as ransom, to spare him the retribution of that day.
70:11.arabic: يبصرونهم يود المجرم لو يفتدي من عذاب يومئذ ببنيه

70:14.text: Even all the people on earth, if it would save him.
70:14.arabic: ومن في الأرض جميعا ثم ينجيه

70:19.text: Indeed, the human being is anxious.

70:23.text: Who always observe their contact prayers (Salat).

70:24.text: Part of their money is set aside.

70:25.text: For the poor and the needy.

70:26.text: They believe in the Day of Judgment.

70:27.arabic: والذين هم من عذاب ربهم مشفقون

70:32.text: And the believers keep their word; they are trustworthy.

70:34.text: They consistently observe their contact prayers (Salat) on time.

70:35.text: They have deserved a position of honor in Paradise.

question: Tell me the story of Yusuf as the Quran narrates it.
answer:
The Joseph (Yūsuf) story is the only sustained, single-prophet narrative in the Quran. Surah 12 — 111 verses, named after him — tells the whole arc from his childhood dream to his father's recovery and his final prayer. The Quran calls it *aḥsanu l-qaṣaṣ* — "the best of stories" ([12:3]).

**1. The dream.**

[12:4]: "Recall that Joseph said to his father, 'O my father, I saw eleven planets, and the sun, and the moon; I saw them prostrating before me.'" — *aḥada `ashara kawkaban wa-sh-shamsa wa-l-qamara ra'aytuhum lī sājidīn*. The boy's vision of *eleven stars and the sun and the moon prostrating to him*.

[12:5]: His father Jacob (Ya`qūb) advises secrecy: "Do not tell your brothers about your dream, lest they plot and scheme against you. Surely, the devil is man's worst enemy."

**2. The brothers' jealousy.**

[12:7]–[12:8]: "In Joseph and his brothers there are lessons for the inquirers. They said, 'Joseph and his brother are favored by our father, and we are in the majority. Our father is plainly in the wrong.'" — the brothers' *resentment* of paternal preference.

[12:10]: One brother proposes a middle course: *do not kill him; throw him into the bottom of the well*.

**3. The well and the caravan.**

[12:15]–[12:21]: They throw him into the well, smear his shirt with goat-blood, return to Jacob with the false story of a wolf. A caravan passing draws him up, takes him to Egypt as a slave, and sells him cheaply.

[12:18]: Jacob, hearing the wolf story: "Patience is my recourse. May GOD help me in the face of your conspiracy."

[12:21]: "The one who bought him in Egypt said to his wife, 'Take good care of him. Maybe he can help us, or maybe we can adopt him.' We thus established Joseph on earth, and we taught him the interpretation of dreams. GOD's command is always done." — the *Egyptian house-holder* (classically the *`Azīz* — chief minister) takes him in. The narrative voice notes: *we taught him the interpretation of dreams*.

**4. The seduction.**

[12:22]: "When he reached maturity, we endowed him with wisdom and knowledge."

[12:23]: "The lady of the house where he lived tried to seduce him. She closed the doors and said, 'I am all yours.' He said, 'May GOD protect me. He is my Lord, who gave me a good home. The transgressors never succeed.'" — *ma`ādha llāh* — *God forbid*. Joseph's *first response* to the seduction is theological.

[12:24]: "She almost succumbed to him, and he almost succumbed to her, if it were not that he saw a proof from his Lord." — *wa-laqad hammat bih wa-hamma bihā law-lā an ra'ā burhāna rabbih*. A theologically dense verse — the Quran does not deny the human pull but specifies the *intervention*: a *proof from his Lord*.

[12:25]: "As the two of them raced towards the door, she tore his garment from the back. They found her husband at the door. She said, 'What should be the punishment for one who wanted to molest your wife, except imprisonment or a painful punishment?'" — the *back-tear* of the shirt — the forensic detail.

[12:26]–[12:27]: A witness from her family proposes a forensic test: *if his shirt is torn from the front, she tells the truth; if from the back, he tells the truth*.

[12:28]: "When her husband saw that his shirt was torn from the back, he said, 'This is a woman's scheme. Indeed, your scheming is formidable.'"

[12:29]: The husband instructs Joseph to *disregard the incident* and asks his wife to *seek forgiveness*. The matter is buried — but the women of the city *hear of it*.

**5. The gathered women — the cut hands.**

[12:30]–[12:31]: City women gossip; the wife invites them to a banquet, gives each a knife, then has Joseph appear. *They cut their own hands* in distraction and say: *this is no human, this is an honorable angel*.

[12:32]: "She said, 'This is the one you blamed me for falling in love with. I did indeed try to seduce him, and he refused. Unless he does what I command him to do, he will be imprisoned, and humiliated.'"

[12:33]: "He said, 'My Lord, the prison is better than giving in to them. Unless You divert their scheming from me, I may desire them and behave like the ignorant ones.'" — Joseph *chooses prison* over compliance.

**6. The prison.**

[12:35]–[12:36]: He is imprisoned. Two fellow inmates ask him to interpret their dreams.

[12:42]: "He then told the one to be saved, 'Remember me at your lord.' Thus, the devil caused him to forget his Lord, and, consequently, he remained in prison a few more years." — Joseph asks the surviving inmate to mention him to the king; the inmate forgets; Joseph remains imprisoned *a few more years*.

**7. The king's dream.**

[12:43]: The king dreams of *seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean*, and *seven green ears of grain* and *seven dried*. His council cannot interpret.

[12:46]–[12:49]: The released inmate remembers Joseph; the king sends for him. Joseph interprets: *seven fat years* of plentiful harvest, *seven lean years* of famine, followed by *a year of relief*. He advises *granary storage* during the fat years.

**8. The rehabilitation.**

[12:50]–[12:53]: Joseph refuses to come out of prison *until his name is cleared*. The king summons the women; the wife confesses: *I tried to seduce him; he is truthful*.

[12:55]: Joseph is offered the *treasury and granaries*; he accepts the responsibility.

**9. The brothers' return.**

[12:58]: "Joseph's brothers came to him; he recognized them, but they did not recognize him." — Joseph, now Egyptian administrator, faces the brothers who *threw him into the well*. They came for grain in the famine.

[12:69]: Joseph privately reveals himself to Benjamin (the *full* brother).

[12:77]: The brothers' second visit, with Benjamin. Joseph engineers Benjamin's retention by *planting his own cup in Benjamin's load*.

[12:80]: The brothers despair; they swear an oath to Jacob *not to return without Benjamin*; they cannot break it.

**10. Jacob's grief — the prayer of patience.**

[12:84]: "He turned away from them, saying, 'I am grieving over Joseph.' His eyes turned white from grieving so much; he was truly sad." — *yā asafā `alā Yūsuf* — *O my grief for Joseph!*

[12:87]: "O my sons, go fetch Joseph and his brother, and never despair of GOD's grace. None despairs of GOD's grace except the disbelieving people." — *lā tay'asū min rawḥi llāh*. The verse is among the Quran's most-cited statements of *non-despair*.

**11. The disclosure.**

[12:89]–[12:90]: "He said, 'Do you recall what you did to Joseph and his brother when you were ignorant?' They said, 'You must be Joseph.' He said, 'I am Joseph, and here is my brother. GOD has blessed us. That is because if one leads a righteous life, and steadfastly perseveres, GOD never fails to reward the righteous.'" — the disclosure. Joseph reads the *theological lesson* of his own story: *righteousness and patience are rewarded*.

[12:91]–[12:92]: The brothers confess: *we were sinful*. Joseph says: *no blame on you today; GOD has forgiven you*.

**12. The shirt.**

[12:93]–[12:96]: Joseph sends his *shirt* (the original shirt or a different one) with the brothers; Jacob *smells Joseph's scent from afar*; the shirt is laid on Jacob's face and his sight returns.

**13. The reunion and the dream fulfilled.**

[12:99]–[12:100]: The family enters Egypt; *they all prostrate before him* — *the dream of [12:4] is now fulfilled*: eleven brothers + father + mother (the eleven stars + sun + moon). Joseph reads the meaning: *my Lord has made it come true*.

**14. The closing prayer.**

[12:101]: "My Lord, You have given me kingship and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Initiator of the heavens and the earth; You are my Lord and Master in this life and in the Hereafter. Let me die as a submitter, and count me with the righteous." — *tawaffanī musliman wa-alḥiqnī bi-ṣ-ṣāliḥīn*. The prayer to *die as a submitter and be joined to the righteous*.

**Synthesis.**

The Joseph story moves: dream → jealousy → well → caravan → adoption → seduction → exoneration → prison → king's dream → rehabilitation → brothers' return → cup-strategy → father's grief and patience-prayer → disclosure → shirt-and-sight → family reunion → dream-fulfilled → closing prayer for death-as-submitter. The narrative covers childhood, slavery, false accusation, prison, rise to power, family reunion, and theological synthesis. The Quran's framing — *the best of stories* ([12:3]) — registers both the *literary completeness* and the *theological richness*: every component (envy, lust, betrayal, false accusation, patient endurance, providential ascent, forgiveness, restoration) is given its full Quranic treatment in a *single sūra*.

12:4.text: Recall that Joseph said to his father, "O my father, I saw eleven planets, and the sun, and the moon; I saw them prostrating before me."
12:4.arabic: إذ قال يوسف لأبيه يـأبت إني رأيت أحد عشر كوكبا والشمس والقمر رأيتهم لي سجدين

12:5.text: He said, "My son, do not tell your brothers about your dream, lest they plot and scheme against you. Surely, the devil is man's worst enemy.
12:5.arabic: قال يبني لا تقصص رءياك على إخوتك فيكيدوا لك كيدا إن الشيطن للإنسن عدو مبين

12:7.text: In Joseph and his brothers there are lessons for the seekers.
12:7.arabic: ۞لقد كان في يوسف وإخوته ءايت للسائلين

12:8.text: They said, "Joseph and his brother are favored by our father, and we are in the majority. Indeed, our father is far astray. Joseph's Fate Already Decided by God
12:8.arabic: إذ قالوا ليوسف وأخوه أحب إلى أبينا منا ونحن عصبة إن أبانا لفي ضلل مبين

12:10.text: One of them said, "Do not kill Joseph; let us throw him into the abyss of the well. Perhaps some caravan can pick him up, if this is what you decide to do."
12:10.arabic: قال قائل منهم لا تقتلوا يوسف وألقوه في غيبت الجب يلتقطه بعض السيارة إن كنتم فعلين

12:15.text: When they went away with him, and unanimously decided to throw him into the abyss of the well, we inspired him: "Some day, you will tell them about all this, while they have no idea."
12:15.arabic: فلما ذهبوا به وأجمعوا أن يجعلوه في غيبت الجب وأوحينا إليه لتنبئنهم بأمرهم هذا وهم لا يشعرون

12:18.text: They produced his shirt with fake blood on it. He said, "Indeed, you have conspired with each other to commit a certain scheme. All I can do is resort to a quiet patience. May GOD help me in the face of your conspiracy." Joseph is Taken to Egypt
12:18.arabic: وجاءو على قميصه بدم كذب قال بل سولت لكم أنفسكم أمرا فصبر جميل والله المستعان على ما تصفون

12:19.text: A caravan passed by, and soon sent their waterer. He let down his bucket, then said, "How lucky! There is a boy here!" They took him along as merchandise, and GOD was fully aware of what they did.
12:19.arabic: وجاءت سيارة فأرسلوا واردهم فأدلى دلوه قال يبشرى هذا غلم وأسروه بضعة والله عليم بما يعملون

12:21.text: The one who bought him in Egypt said to his wife, "Take good care of him. Maybe he can help us, or maybe we can adopt him." We thus established Joseph on earth, and we taught him the interpretation of dreams. GOD's command is always done, but most people do not know.
12:21.arabic: وقال الذي اشترىه من مصر لامرأته أكرمي مثوىه عسى أن ينفعنا أو نتخذه ولدا وكذلك مكنا ليوسف في الأرض ولنعلمه من تأويل الأحاديث والله غالب على أمره ولكن أكثر الناس لا يعلمون

12:22.text: When he reached maturity, we endowed him with wisdom and knowledge. We thus reward the righteous.
12:22.arabic: ولما بلغ أشده ءاتينه حكما وعلما وكذلك نجزي المحسنين

12:23.text: The lady of the house where he lived tried to seduce him. She closed the doors and said, "I am all yours." He said, "May GOD protect me. He is my Lord, who gave me a good home. The transgressors never succeed."
12:23.arabic: ورودته التي هو في بيتها عن نفسه وغلقت الأبوب وقالت هيت لك قال معاذ الله إنه ربي أحسن مثواي إنه لا يفلح الظـلمون

12:24.text: She almost succumbed to him, and he almost succumbed to her, if it were not that he saw a proof from his Lord. We thus diverted evil and sin away from him, for he was one of our devoted servants.
12:24.arabic: ولقد همت به وهم بها لولا أن رءا برهن ربه كذلك لنصرف عنه السوء والفحشاء إنه من عبادنا المخلصين

12:25.text: The two of them raced towards the door, and, in the process, she tore his garment from the back. They found her husband at the door. She said, "What should be the punishment for one who wanted to molest your wife, except imprisonment or a painful punishment?"
12:25.arabic: واستبقا الباب وقدت قميصه من دبر وألفيا سيدها لدا الباب قالت ما جزاء من أراد بأهلك سوءا إلا أن يسجن أو عذاب أليم

12:26.text: He said, "She is the one who tried to seduce me." A witness from her family suggested: "If his garment is torn from the front, then she is telling the truth and he is a liar.
12:26.arabic: قال هي رودتني عن نفسي وشهد شاهد من أهلها إن كان قميصه قد من قبل فصدقت وهو من الكذبين

12:27.text: "And if his garment is torn from the back, then she lied, and he is telling the truth."
12:27.arabic: وإن كان قميصه قد من دبر فكذبت وهو من الصـدقين

12:28.text: When her husband saw that his garment was torn from the back, he said, "This is a woman's scheme. Indeed, your scheming is formidable.
12:28.arabic: فلما رءا قميصه قد من دبر قال إنه من كيدكن إن كيدكن عظيم

12:29.text: "Joseph, disregard this incident. As for you (my wife), you should seek forgiveness for your sin. You have committed an error."
12:29.arabic: يوسف أعرض عن هذا واستغفري لذنبك إنك كنت من الخاطـين

12:30.text: Some women in the city gossiped: "The governor's wife is trying to seduce her servant. She is deeply in love with him. We see that she has gone astray."
12:30.arabic: ۞وقال نسوة في المدينة امرأت العزيز ترود فتىها عن نفسه قد شغفها حبا إنا لنرىها في ضلل مبين

12:31.text: When she heard of their gossip, she invited them, prepared for them a comfortable place, and gave each of them a knife. She then said to him, "Enter their room." When they saw him, they so admired him, that they cut their hands. They said, "Glory be to GOD, this is not a human being; this is an honorable angel."
12:31.arabic: فلما سمعت بمكرهن أرسلت إليهن وأعتدت لهن متكـا وءاتت كل وحدة منهن سكينا وقالت اخرج عليهن فلما رأينه أكبرنه وقطعن أيديهن وقلن حش لله ما هذا بشرا إن هذا إلا ملك كريم

12:32.text: She said, "This is the one you blamed me for falling in love with. I did indeed try to seduce him, and he refused. Unless he does what I command him to do, he will surely go to prison, and will be debased."
12:32.arabic: قالت فذلكن الذي لمتنني فيه ولقد رودته عن نفسه فاستعصم ولئن لم يفعل ما ءامره ليسجنن وليكونا من الصـغرين

12:33.text: He said, "My Lord, the prison is better than giving in to them. Unless You divert their scheming from me, I may desire them and behave like the ignorant ones."
12:33.arabic: قال رب السجن أحب إلي مما يدعونني إليه وإلا تصرف عني كيدهن أصب إليهن وأكن من الجهلين

12:35.text: Later, they saw to it, despite the clear proofs, that they should imprison him for awhile.
12:35.arabic: ثم بدا لهم من بعد ما رأوا الأيت ليسجننه حتى حين

12:36.text: Two young men were in the prison with him. One of them said, "I saw (in my dream) that I was making wine," and the other said, "I saw myself carrying bread on my head, from which the birds were eating. Inform us of the interpretation of these dreams. We see that you are righteous."
12:36.arabic: ودخل معه السجن فتيان قال أحدهما إني أرىني أعصر خمرا وقال الأخر إني أرىني أحمل فوق رأسي خبزا تأكل الطير منه نبئنا بتأويله إنا نرىك من المحسنين

12:42.text: He then said to the one to be saved "Remember me at your lord." Thus, the devil caused him to forget his Lord, and, consequently, he remained in prison a few more years.
12:42.arabic: وقال للذي ظن أنه ناج منهما اذكرني عند ربك فأنسىه الشيطن ذكر ربه فلبث في السجن بضع سنين

12:43.text: The king said, "I saw seven fat cows being devoured by seven skinny cows, and seven green spikes (of wheat), and others shriveled. O my elders, advise me regarding my dream, if you know how to interpret the dreams."
12:43.arabic: وقال الملك إني أرى سبع بقرت سمان يأكلهن سبع عجاف وسبع سنبلت خضر وأخر يابست يـأيها الملأ أفتوني في رءيي إن كنتم للرءيا تعبرون

12:46.text: "Joseph my friend, inform us about seven fat cows being devoured by seven skinny cows, and seven green spikes, and others shriveled. I wish to go back with some information for the people."
12:46.arabic: يوسف أيها الصديق أفتنا في سبع بقرت سمان يأكلهن سبع عجاف وسبع سنبلت خضر وأخر يابست لعلي أرجع إلى الناس لعلهم يعلمون

12:55.text: He said, "Make me the treasurer, for I am experienced in this area and knowledgeable."
12:55.arabic: قال اجعلني على خزائن الأرض إني حفيظ عليم

12:58.text: Joseph's brothers came; when they entered, he recognized them, while they did not recognize him.
12:58.arabic: وجاء إخوة يوسف فدخلوا عليه فعرفهم وهم له منكرون

12:69.text: When they entered Joseph's place, he brought his brother closer to him and said, "I am your brother; do not be saddened by their actions."
12:69.arabic: ولما دخلوا على يوسف ءاوى إليه أخاه قال إني أنا أخوك فلا تبتئس بما كانوا يعملون

12:77.text: They said, "If he stole, so did a brother of his in the past." Joseph concealed his feelings in himself, and did not give them any clue. He said (to himself), "You are really bad. GOD is fully aware of your accusations."
12:77.arabic: ۞قالوا إن يسرق فقد سرق أخ له من قبل فأسرها يوسف في نفسه ولم يبدها لهم قال أنتم شر مكانا والله أعلم بما تصفون

12:80.text: When they despaired of changing his mind, they conferred together. Their eldest said, "Do you realize that your father has taken a solemn pledge from you before GOD? In the past you lost Joseph. I am not leaving this place until my father gives me permission, or until GOD judges for me; He is the best Judge.
12:80.arabic: فلما استيـسوا منه خلصوا نجيا قال كبيرهم ألم تعلموا أن أباكم قد أخذ عليكم موثقا من الله ومن قبل ما فرطتم في يوسف فلن أبرح الأرض حتى يأذن لي أبي أو يحكم الله لي وهو خير الحكمين

12:84.text: He turned away from them, saying, "I am grieving over Joseph." His eyes turned white from grieving so much; he was truly sad.
12:84.arabic: وتولى عنهم وقال يـأسفى على يوسف وابيضت عيناه من الحزن فهو كظيم

12:87.text: "O my sons, go fetch Joseph and his brother, and never despair of GOD's grace. None despairs of GOD's grace except the disbelieving people."
12:87.arabic: يبني اذهبوا فتحسسوا من يوسف وأخيه ولا تايـسوا من روح الله إنه لا يايـس من روح الله إلا القوم الكفرون

12:89.text: He said, "Do you recall what you did to Joseph and his brother when you were ignorant?"
12:89.arabic: قال هل علمتم ما فعلتم بيوسف وأخيه إذ أنتم جهلون

12:90.text: They said, "You must be Joseph." He said, "I am Joseph, and here is my brother. GOD has blessed us. That is because if one leads a righteous life, and steadfastly perseveres, GOD never fails to reward the righteous."
12:90.arabic: قالوا أءنك لأنت يوسف قال أنا يوسف وهذا أخي قد من الله علينا إنه من يتق ويصبر فإن الله لا يضيع أجر المحسنين

12:96.text: When the bearer of good news arrived, he threw (the shirt) on his face, whereupon his vision was restored. He said, "Did I not tell you that I knew from GOD what you did not know?"
12:96.arabic: فلما أن جاء البشير ألقىه على وجهه فارتد بصيرا قال ألم أقل لكم إني أعلم من الله ما لا تعلمون

12:99.text: When they entered Joseph's quarters, he embraced his parents, saying, "Welcome to Egypt. GOD willing, you will be safe here."
12:99.arabic: فلما دخلوا على يوسف ءاوى إليه أبويه وقال ادخلوا مصر إن شاء الله ءامنين

12:100.text: He raised his parents upon the throne. They fell prostrate before him. He said, "O my father, this is the fulfillment of my old dream. My Lord has made it come true. He has blessed me, delivered me from the prison, and brought you from the desert, after the devil had driven a wedge between me and my brothers. My Lord is Most Kind towards whomever He wills. He is the Knower, the Most Wise."
12:100.arabic: ورفع أبويه على العرش وخروا له سجدا وقال يـأبت هذا تأويل رءيي من قبل قد جعلها ربي حقا وقد أحسن بي إذ أخرجني من السجن وجاء بكم من البدو من بعد أن نزغ الشيطن بيني وبين إخوتي إن ربي لطيف لما يشاء إنه هو العليم الحكيم

12:101.text: "My Lord, You have given me kingship and taught me the interpretation of dreams. Initiator of the heavens and the earth; You are my Lord and Master in this life and in the Hereafter. Let me die as a submitter, and count me with the righteous."
12:101.arabic: ۞رب قد ءاتيتني من الملك وعلمتني من تأويل الأحاديث فاطر السموت والأرض أنت ولي في الدنيا والأخرة توفني مسلما وألحقني بالصـلحين

question: What is the central message of Surah 78 (The Event)?
answer:
Surah 78 (An-Naba' — "The Event" / "The News") is forty verses, Meccan. The surah opens with the Quraysh's *disputatious questioning* about the Day, demolishes the doubt with a *seven-fold creation-sign sequence*, and closes with the *two-face panel* (paradise for the reverent, hell for the disbelieving) and the warning that on that day *the disbeliever will wish he were dust*.

**The opening — what are they disputing?**

[78:1]–[78:3]: "What are they disputing? The great event. That is the subject of their dispute." — *`amma yatasā'alūn / `ani n-naba'i l-`aẓīm / alladhī hum fīhi mukhtalifūn*. *The great news* — *an-naba' al-`aẓīm* — gives the surah its title. The disbelievers were *disputing about it among themselves* — some inclining toward belief, some toward denial, some confused. The surah opens by *identifying their argument*.

[78:4]–[78:5]: "Indeed, they will find out. Most assuredly, they will find out." — *kallā sa-ya`lamūn / thumma kallā sa-ya`lamūn*. *No — they will know. Then — no — they will know.* The doubled-emphatic *kallā* + double promise of knowledge is one of the strongest *certainty-affirmations* in the short Meccan surahs.

**The seven creation-signs.**

The surah's structural center is a *sequence of seven creation-claims*, each presented as a rhetorical *did we not*:

[78:6]: "Did we not make the earth habitable?" — *a-lam naj`ali l-arḍa mihādā*. *Mihād* — "a couch, a cradle." The earth as *prepared bed*.

[78:7]: "And the mountains stabilizers?" — *wa-l-jibāla awtādā*. *Awtād* — "pegs, stakes." The mountains *as pegs* — a recurring Quranic image (cross-reference [21:31], [16:15]).

[78:8]: "We created you as mates (for one another)." — *wa-khalaqnākum azwājā*. *Pairs*. Creation in *gender pairs*.

[78:9]: "We made your sleep a slumber." — *wa-ja`alnā nawmakum subātā*. *Subāt* — "cutting off, complete rest." The verse is the only Quranic discussion of *sleep as biological function*.

[78:10]: "We made the night a cover." — *wa-ja`alnā l-layla libāsā*. *Libās* — "clothing, cover." Night as *garment*.

[78:11]: "And the day for livelihood." — *wa-ja`alnā n-nahāra ma`āshā*. Day as *time for earning*.

[78:12]: "We built above you seven mighty (universes)." — *wa-banaynā fawqakum sab`an shidādā*. Seven *strong* heavens.

[78:13]: "We created a bright lamp." — *wa-ja`alnā sirājan wahhājā*. *Sirāj wahhāj* — *blazing lamp* — the sun.

[78:14]–[78:16]: "And from the clouds we send pouring water. To produce with it grains and plants. And various lush gardens." — the *rain-cycle* and its agricultural product. Three nested verses: *clouds → rain → grains and plants → lush gardens*.

The creation-roster: earth as bed, mountains as pegs, gender pairs, sleep as rest, night as cover, day as livelihood, sky as seven strong domes, sun as lamp, rain → grain → garden. Seventeen creation-claims condensed into eleven verses. The argument: *if all of this is real and given, the Day is real and coming*.

**The Day's announcement.**

[78:17]: "The Day of Decision is appointed." — *inna yawma l-faṣli kāna mīqātā*. *The Day of Faṣl (Decision/Separation) is an appointed time*.

[78:18]: "The day the horn is blown, and you come in throngs." — *yawma yunfakhu fī ṣ-ṣūri fa-ta'tūna afwājā*.

[78:19]–[78:20]: "The heaven will be opened like gates. The mountains will be removed as if they were a mirage." — *suyyirati l-jibālu fa-kānat sarābā*. *The mountains move, they become a mirage* — the *substantial* dissolves.

**The hell-arrival.**

[78:21]–[78:30]: Hell awaits the transgressors; *they will not taste coolness or drink*, only *scalding water and bitter food* — *just requital for their works*.

[78:25]: "Only boiling drinks and bitter food."

**The paradise.**

[78:31]–[78:36]: The reverent (*muttaqūn*) have a triumph: gardens, vineyards, female companions of equal age, full cups, no vain talk, no lies — *a reward from your Lord, generous payment* — *a reward proportionate to the works* (*jazā'an min rabbika `aṭā'an ḥisābā*).

**The Spirit and the angels.**

[78:37]–[78:38]: "Lord of the heavens and the earth, and everything between them, the Most Gracious. No one can abrogate His decisions. The day will come when the Spirit and the angels will stand in a row. None will speak except those permitted by the Most Gracious, and they will speak only what is right." — *yawma yaqūmu r-rūḥu wa-l-malā'ikatu ṣaffā*. *On the day the Spirit and the angels stand in a row*. The *Spirit* (*ar-Rūḥ*) is named with a definite article, suggesting *a specific being* (classical exegesis: Gabriel; some Khalifa-aligned readings: the divine spirit). *None will speak except whom the Most Gracious permits*.

**The disbeliever's wish.**

[78:40]: "We have sufficiently warned you about an imminent retribution. That is the day when everyone will examine what his hands have sent forth, and the disbeliever will say, 'Oh, I wish I were dust.'" — *yā laytanī kuntu turābā*. The disbeliever's *post-mortem wish*: *would that I were dust* — the same dust he *thought he would return to and then nothing more*.

**Central message in one sentence.**

The Day is *the great news* the disbelievers are disputing ([78:1]–[78:3]); the *did-we-not* sequence — earth as bed, mountains as pegs, pairs, sleep, night, day, seven domes, sun-lamp, rain, grain, garden ([78:6]–[78:16]) — *guarantees* the Day's reality; the horn is blown, the heaven opens, mountains mirage ([78:18]–[78:20]); hell waits the transgressors, paradise the reverent ([78:21]–[78:36]); the Spirit and angels stand in a row, none speaks unpermitted ([78:38]); and the disbeliever wishes he were dust ([78:40]).
